_meta.description: Cumulative SC Precalculus question bank — one section added per run. Import each section separately or the full file.

question_types.title: Evaluating Functions with Algebraic Inputs
question_types.mapping_rationale: Evaluating a function at an algebraic input such as x - 1 primarily tests substitution and correct interpretation of algebraic structure. The task also reinforces attention to valid function inputs and domain considerations.

questions.question: Evaluate the function at the specified value of the independent variable and simplify: $f(x) = 2x - 3$. Find $f(x-1)$.
Evaluate the function at the specified value of the independent variable and simplify: $g(y) = 7 - 3y$. Find $g(s+2)$.
Evaluate the function at the specified value of the independent variable and simplify: $f(x) = \sqrt{x+8} + 2$. Find $f(x-8)$.
questions.explanation: Substitute $(x - 8)$ for $x$: $f(x - 8) = \sqrt{(x - 8) + 8} + 2 = \sqrt{x} + 2$.

question_types.title: Evaluating Piecewise-Defined Functions
question_types.mapping_rationale: Evaluating a piecewise-defined function requires matching each input to the appropriate restricted interval and then evaluating the corresponding algebraic rule correctly.

questions.question: Evaluate the piecewise function at the given values: $f(x) = \begin{cases} 3x-4, & x < 0 \\ 3x+1, & x \ge 0 \end{cases}$. Find $f(-2)$, $f(0)$, and $f(2)$.
questions.explanation: For $x = -2$, use $3x - 4$ because $-2 < 0$, giving $-10$. For $x = 0$ and $x = 2$, use $3x + 1$ because those inputs satisfy $x \ge 0$, giving $1$ and $7$.

questions.question: Evaluate the piecewise function at the given values: $f(x) = \begin{cases} 5-2x, & x < 0 \\ 5, & x \ge 0 \end{cases}$. Find $f(-2)$, $f(0)$, and $f(2)$.
questions.explanation: For $x = -2$, use $5 - 2x$ because $-2 < 0$, giving $9$. For $x = 0$ and $x = 2$, use the constant rule $5$ because those inputs satisfy $x \ge 0$.

questions.question: Evaluate the piecewise function at the given values: $q(x) = \begin{cases} \frac{1}{x^2-4}, & x \le 0 \\ \frac{1}{x^2+4}, & x > 0 \end{cases}$. Find $q(-1)$, $q(0)$, and $q(1)$.
questions.explanation: For $x = -1$ and $x = 0$, use $\frac{1}{x^2-4}$ because those inputs satisfy $x \le 0$, giving $-\frac{1}{3}$ and $-\frac{1}{4}$. For $x = 1$, use $\frac{1}{x^2+4}$ because $x > 0$, giving $\frac{1}{5}$.

question_types.title: Finding the Domain of a Function Algebraically
question_types.mapping_rationale: Determining domain directly targets valid-input reasoning, while identifying nonzero-denominator and nonnegative-radicand constraints depends on analyzing algebraic structure correctly.

questions.question: Find the domain of the function: $f(x) = 5x^2 + 2x - 1$
questions.explanation: The function is a polynomial, so it is defined for all real numbers. Its domain is $(-\infty, \infty)$.

questions.question: Find the domain of the function: $h(t) = \frac{4}{t}$
questions.explanation: A rational function is undefined when its denominator is zero. Since $t = 0$ makes the denominator zero, the domain is all real numbers except $0$.

questions.question: Find the domain of the function: $g(y) = \sqrt{y - 10}$
questions.explanation: For a square root to be real, the radicand must be nonnegative. Solve $y - 10 \ge 0$ to get $y \ge 10$.

question_types.title: Finding Values Where f(x) = g(x)
question_types.mapping_rationale: Setting two functions equal and solving for intersections relies on rewriting and factoring algebraic expressions, while also reinforcing equation-solving in contexts that represent equal outputs or intersections.

questions.question: Find all values of $x$ such that $f(x) = g(x)$ for the functions: $f(x) = x^2 + 2x + 1$ and $g(x) = 3x + 3$.
questions.explanation: Set the functions equal: $x^2 + 2x + 1 = 3x + 3$. Move all terms to one side: $x^2 - x - 2 = 0$. Factor: $(x - 2)(x + 1) = 0$, so $x = 2$ or $x = -1$.

questions.question: Find all values of $x$ such that $f(x) = g(x)$ for the functions: $f(x) = x^4 - 2x^2$ and $g(x) = 2x^2$.
questions.explanation: Set the functions equal: $x^4 - 2x^2 = 2x^2$. Then $x^4 - 4x^2 = 0$. Factor: $x^2(x^2 - 4) = 0$, so $x = 0$ or $x = \pm 2$.

questions.question: Find all values of $x$ such that $f(x) = g(x)$ for the functions: $f(x) = \sqrt{3x} + 1$ and $g(x) = x + 1$.
questions.explanation: Set the functions equal: $\sqrt{3x} + 1 = x + 1$. Then $\sqrt{3x} = x$. Square both sides: $3x = x^2$, so $x^2 - 3x = 0$. Factor: $x(x - 3) = 0$, giving $x = 0$ or $x = 3$. Both check in the original equation.

question_types.title: Finding the Domain of a Function Algebraically
question_types.mapping_rationale: Determining the domain from a symbolic expression is PC.FIF.5.1. PC.FIF.4.1 (different parent LO PC.FIF.4) provides reinforcement via key-feature interpretation; PC.ASE.1.2 is needed to identify problematic denominators and radicands.

questions.question: Find the domain of the function: $f(x) = 5x^2 + 2x - 1$
questions.explanation: The function $f(x) = 5x^2 + 2x - 1$ is a polynomial. Polynomials are defined for all real numbers, so there are no restrictions on the domain. Thus, the domain is $(-\infty, \infty)$.

questions.question: Find the domain of the function: $g(x) = \frac{1}{x} - \frac{3}{x+2}$
options: A) All real numbers except $x = 0$ and $x = -2$
B) All real numbers except $x = 0$ and $x = 2$
questions.correct_option: A) All real numbers except $x = 0$ and $x = -2$
questions.answer: A) All real numbers except $x = 0$ and $x = -2$
questions.explanation: The domain of a rational function excludes values that make any denominator zero. Setting $x = 0$ and $x + 2 = 0$ gives the excluded values $x = 0$ and $x = -2$.

questions.question: Find the domain of the function: $h(x) = \sqrt{x - 4}$
questions.explanation: For the square root to be real, the radicand must be non-negative: $x - 4 \ge 0 \Rightarrow x \ge 4$.

question_types.title: Finding the Zeros of a Function Algebraically
question_types.mapping_rationale: Finding zeros algebraically supports identifying x-intercepts as key features in graphing (PC.FIF.7.1). PC.FIF.7.2 is aligned (same parent LO PC.FIF.7) since one question involves a rational function. Factoring polynomial structure is the core algebraic technique (PC.ASE.2.1).

questions.question: Find the zeros of the function algebraically: $f(x) = 2x^2 - 7x - 30$
Find the zeros of the function algebraically: $f(x) = \frac{x}{9x^2 - 4}$
questions.explanation: A rational function is zero when its numerator is zero and denominator is non-zero. Numerator $x = 0$; denominator at $x = 0$: $9(0)^2 - 4 = -4 \ne 0$. So $x = 0$ is the only zero.

questions.question: Find the zeros of the function algebraically: $f(x) = \frac{1}{2}x^3 - x$
questions.explanation: Factor: $x\left(\frac{1}{2}x^2 - 1\right) = 0$. So $x = 0$ or $\frac{1}{2}x^2 = 1 \Rightarrow x^2 = 2 \Rightarrow x = \pm\sqrt{2}$.

question_types.title: Calculating Average Rate of Change Algebraically
question_types.mapping_rationale: Applying the average rate of change formula over a given interval is PC.FIF.6.1. PC.FIF.6.2 is aligned (same parent LO PC.FIF.6) — students implicitly interpret rate as slope. Correct function evaluation requires expression interpretation (PC.ASE.1.2).

questions.question: Find the average rate of change of the function from $x_1$ to $x_2$: $f(x) = -2x + 15$, $x_1 = 0,\ x_2 = 3$
questions.explanation: $\frac{f(3) - f(0)}{3 - 0} = \frac{9 - 15}{3} = \frac{-6}{3} = -2$. For a linear function this equals the slope.

questions.question: Find the average rate of change of the function from $x_1$ to $x_2$: $f(x) = x^2 - 2x + 8$, $x_1 = 1,\ x_2 = 5$
Find the average rate of change of the function from $x_1$ to $x_2$: $f(x) = x^3 - 3x^2 - x$, $x_1 = -1,\ x_2 = 2$

question_types.title: Determining Even and Odd Functions Algebraically
question_types.mapping_rationale: Testing parity algebraically defines symmetry about axes (PC.FIF.4.2). PC.FIF.4.1 is aligned (same parent LO PC.FIF.4) — symmetry is a key feature of function graphs. Correct substitution of $-x$ requires interpreting expression structure (PC.ASE.1.2).

questions.question: Determine whether the function is even, odd, or neither: $f(x) = x^6 - 2x^2 + 3$
questions.explanation: $f(-x) = (-x)^6 - 2(-x)^2 + 3 = x^6 - 2x^2 + 3 = f(x)$. Since $f(-x) = f(x)$, the function is even.

questions.question: Determine whether the function is even, odd, or neither: $h(x) = x^3 - 5x$
questions.explanation: $h(-x) = (-x)^3 - 5(-x) = -x^3 + 5x = -(x^3 - 5x) = -h(x)$. Since $h(-x) = -h(x)$, the function is odd.

questions.question: Determine whether the function is even, odd, or neither: $g(x) = x\sqrt{1 - x^2}$
questions.explanation: $g(-x) = (-x)\sqrt{1 - (-x)^2} = -x\sqrt{1 - x^2} = -g(x)$. Since $g(-x) = -g(x)$, the function is odd.

question_types.title: Writing Linear Functions from Function Values
question_types.mapping_rationale: Constructing the equation of a linear function from given function values directly assesses PC.FLQE.2.1. Interpreting function notation as ordered-pair information and assembling the resulting expression draws on algebraic structure under PC.ASE.1.2.

questions.question: Write the linear function $f$ such that it has the indicated function values: $f(1) = 4, f(0) = 6$
questions.explanation: The function values correspond to the points $(1, 4)$ and $(0, 6)$. The slope is $m = \frac{6 - 4}{0 - 1} = -2$, and the y-intercept is $f(0) = 6$, so $f(x) = -2x + 6$.

questions.question: Write the linear function $f$ such that it has the indicated function values: $f(-3) = -8, f(1) = 2$
questions.explanation: The points are $(-3, -8)$ and $(1, 2)$. The slope is $m = \frac{2 - (-8)}{1 - (-3)} = \frac{10}{4} = \frac{5}{2}$. Using point-slope form and simplifying gives $f(x) = \frac{5}{2}x - \frac{1}{2}$.

questions.question: Write the linear function $f$ such that it has the indicated function values: $f(5) = -4, f(-2) = 17$
questions.explanation: The points are $(5, -4)$ and $(-2, 17)$. The slope is $m = \frac{17 - (-4)}{-2 - 5} = \frac{21}{-7} = -3$. Substituting into point-slope form and simplifying gives $f(x) = -3x + 11$.

question_types.title: Evaluating Piecewise-Defined Functions
question_types.mapping_rationale: Evaluating a piecewise-defined function requires first determining which input interval applies, so valid-input interpretation is central. Once the correct piece is selected, students must interpret and evaluate the algebraic structure of that rule.

questions.question: Evaluate the function $f(x) = \begin{cases} 2x+1, & x < 0 \\ 2x+2, & x \ge 0 \end{cases}$ for the values $f(-1)$, $f(0)$, and $f(2)$.
questions.explanation: For $x = -1$, use $2x+1$ because $x < 0$, giving $-1$. For $x = 0$ and $x = 2$, use $2x+2$ because those inputs satisfy $x \ge 0$, giving $2$ and $6$.

questions.question: Evaluate the function $f(x) = \begin{cases} x^2+2, & x \le 1 \\ 2x^2+2, & x > 1 \end{cases}$ for the values $f(-2)$, $f(1)$, and $f(2)$.
questions.explanation: For $x = -2$ and $x = 1$, use $x^2+2$ because those inputs satisfy $x \le 1$, giving $6$ and $3$. For $x = 2$, use $2x^2+2$ because $x > 1$, giving $10$.

questions.question: Evaluate the function $f(x) = \begin{cases} 3x-1, & x < -1 \\ 4, & -1 \le x \le 1 \\ x^2, & x > 1 \end{cases}$ for the values $f(-2)$, $f(-\frac{1}{2})$, and $f(3)$.
questions.explanation: For $x = -2$, use $3x-1$ because $x < -1$, giving $-7$. For $x = -\frac{1}{2}$, use the middle constant piece because $-1 \le x \le 1$, giving $4$. For $x = 3$, use $x^2$ because $x > 1$, giving $9$.

question_types.title: Identifying Graph Characteristics of Piecewise Functions
question_types.mapping_rationale: These items ask students to analyze the visible behavior and features of piecewise-defined graphs, including endpoints, continuity, symmetry, and notable extrema. This is most directly a graph-interpretation task, with additional reinforcement from connecting symbolic piecewise rules to their graphical output.

questions.question: Which of the following describes the key characteristics of the graph of the piecewise function $g(x) = \begin{cases} x+6, & x \le -4 \\ \frac{1}{2}x-4, & x > -4 \end{cases}$?
options: A) It consists of a ray ending at the solid point $(-4, 2)$ and a second ray starting at the open point $(-4, -6)$.
B) It is a continuous V-shaped graph with its vertex at $(-4, 2)$.
C) It consists of a ray ending at the open point $(-4, 2)$ and a second ray starting at the solid point $(-4, -6)$.
D) It is a horizontal line on $x \le -4$ and a diagonal line on $x > -4$.
questions.correct_option: A) It consists of a ray ending at the solid point $(-4, 2)$ and a second ray starting at the open point $(-4, -6)$.
questions.answer: A) It consists of a ray ending at the solid point $(-4, 2)$ and a second ray starting at the open point $(-4, -6)$.
questions.explanation: For the piece $x+6$ on $x \le -4$, substituting $x = -4$ gives the solid point $(-4, 2)$. For the piece $\frac{1}{2}x-4$ on $x > -4$, substituting $x = -4$ gives the open point $(-4, -6)$.

questions.question: Which of the following describes the key characteristics of the graph of the piecewise function $f(x) = \begin{cases} \sqrt{4+x}, & x < 0 \\ \sqrt{4-x}, & x \ge 0 \end{cases}$?
options: A) The graph is symmetric about the y-axis with a domain of $[-4, 4]$, meeting at a peak of $(0,2)$.
B) The graph consists of two full parabolas opening upwards.
C) The graph is discontinuous at $x = 0$ with a jump between $y = -2$ and $y = 2$.
D) The graph is a continuous straight line from $(-4, 0)$ to $(4, 0)$.
questions.correct_option: A) The graph is symmetric about the y-axis with a domain of $[-4, 4]$, meeting at a peak of $(0,2)$.
questions.answer: A) The graph is symmetric about the y-axis with a domain of $[-4, 4]$, meeting at a peak of $(0,2)$.
questions.explanation: The first piece requires $x \ge -4$ and the second requires $x \le 4$, so together the domain is $[-4, 4]$. Both pieces meet at $(0, 2)$, forming a continuous graph that is symmetric about the y-axis.

questions.question: Which of the following describes the key characteristics of the graph of the piecewise function $f(x) = \begin{cases} 1-(x-1)^2, & x \le 2 \\ \sqrt{x-2}, & x > 2 \end{cases}$?
options: A) The graph is continuous at $x = 2$ and has a local maximum at $(1, 1)$.
B) The graph is discontinuous at $x = 2$ with a jump from $y = 0$ to $y = 1$.
C) The graph is a straight line until $x = 2$, then curves upward.
D) The graph has a local minimum at $(1, 1)$ and an open hole at $(2, 0)$.
questions.correct_option: A) The graph is continuous at $x = 2$ and has a local maximum at $(1, 1)$.
questions.answer: A) The graph is continuous at $x = 2$ and has a local maximum at $(1, 1)$.
questions.explanation: The first piece is a downward-opening parabola with vertex $(1, 1)$, so it has a local maximum there. At $x = 2$, the left piece gives $0$, and the right-hand square-root piece approaches $0$ as $x \to 2^+$, so the graph is continuous at $x = 2$.

question_types.title: Describing Transformations from Algebraic Rules
question_types.mapping_rationale: Identifying the geometric transformations directly assesses PC.FBF.3.1, while mapping those effects relies heavily on successfully parsing the structure of the algebraic expression (PC.ASE.1.2).

questions.question: Identify the parent function $f$ and describe the sequence of transformations from $f$ to $g$ for the function: $g(x) = x^2 + 6$
options: C) $f(x) = x^2$; shifted 6 units to the right
D) $f(x) = x^2$; shifted 6 units to the left
questions.explanation: The parent function is the squaring function, $f(x) = x^2$. Adding a positive constant outside the function rule, $+ 6$, results in a vertical translation upward by 6 units.

questions.question: Identify the parent function $f$ and describe the sequence of transformations from $f$ to $g$ for the function: $g(x) = -(x - 2)^3$
options: A) $f(x) = x^3$; shifted 2 units right, then reflected in the x-axis
B) $f(x) = x^3$; shifted 2 units left, then reflected in the x-axis
C) $f(x) = x^3$; shifted 2 units right, then reflected in the y-axis
D) $f(x) = x^2$; shifted 2 units right, then reflected in the x-axis
questions.correct_option: A) $f(x) = x^3$; shifted 2 units right, then reflected in the x-axis
questions.answer: A) $f(x) = x^3$; shifted 2 units right, then reflected in the x-axis
questions.explanation: The parent function is the cubic function, $f(x) = x^3$. The subtraction inside the function, $(x - 2)$, shifts the graph 2 units to the right. The negative sign outside the function indicates a vertical reflection across the x-axis.

questions.question: Identify the parent function $f$ and describe the sequence of transformations from $f$ to $g$ for the function: $g(x) = -|x| - 4$
options: A) $f(x) = |x|$; reflected in the x-axis, then shifted 4 units downward
B) $f(x) = |x|$; reflected in the y-axis, then shifted 4 units downward
C) $f(x) = |x|$; shifted 4 units left, then reflected in the x-axis
D) $f(x) = |x|$; reflected in the x-axis, then shifted 4 units upward
questions.correct_option: A) $f(x) = |x|$; reflected in the x-axis, then shifted 4 units downward
questions.answer: A) $f(x) = |x|$; reflected in the x-axis, then shifted 4 units downward
questions.explanation: The parent function is the absolute value function, $f(x) = |x|$. The negative sign outside the absolute value causes a reflection in the x-axis. The $-4$ outside the function causes a vertical shift downward by 4 units.

questions.question: Identify the parent function $f$ and describe the sequence of transformations from $f$ to $g$ for the function: $g(x) = |x - 1| + 2$
options: A) $f(x) = |x|$; shifted 1 unit right and 2 units upward
B) $f(x) = |x|$; shifted 1 unit left and 2 units upward
C) $f(x) = |x|$; shifted 1 unit right and 2 units downward
D) $f(x) = x^2$; shifted 1 unit right and 2 units upward
questions.correct_option: A) $f(x) = |x|$; shifted 1 unit right and 2 units upward
questions.answer: A) $f(x) = |x|$; shifted 1 unit right and 2 units upward
questions.explanation: The parent function is the absolute value function, $f(x) = |x|$. The $(x - 1)$ inside the function shifts the graph 1 unit to the right. The $+2$ outside the function shifts it 2 units upward.

question_types.title: Writing Equations from Verbal Transformation Descriptions
question_types.mapping_rationale: Writing an equation based on transformation descriptions targets PC.FBF.3.2, relying on understanding what the individual parameter changes do geometrically (PC.FBF.3.1) and assembling them logically into an algebraic expression (PC.ASE.1.2).

questions.question: Write a rule for $g$ that represents the indicated transformations of the graph of $f$: $f(x) = x^3$, translation 2 units right and 5 units down.
questions.explanation: A translation 2 units to the right requires subtracting 2 inside the base function, yielding $(x - 2)^3$. A translation 5 units down requires subtracting 5 from the overall function. This gives $g(x) = (x - 2)^3 - 5$.

questions.question: Write a rule for $g$ that represents the indicated transformations of the graph of $f$: $f(x) = |x|$, reflection in the x-axis and translation 4 units left.
questions.explanation: A reflection in the x-axis requires placing a negative sign outside the function: $-|x|$. A translation 4 units left requires adding 4 inside the absolute value brackets. This yields $g(x) = -|x + 4|$.

questions.question: Write a rule for $g$ that represents the indicated transformations of the graph of $f$: $f(x) = |x|$, translation 2 units down and reflection in the y-axis.
questions.explanation: A reflection in the y-axis replaces $x$ with $-x$ inside the function, giving $|-x|$. A translation 2 units down requires subtracting 2 from the entire function. Combining these yields $g(x) = |-x| - 2$. Since $|-x| = |x|$, this graph is identical to $|x| - 2$.

question_types.title: Identifying the Equation of a Transformed Graph
question_types.mapping_rationale: Writing an equation corresponding to a given graph explicitly tests PC.FBF.3.2. Success depends on visually interpreting key features such as vertex, opening direction, and intercept-related cues from the graph (PC.FIF.4.1) to identify the transformation parameters.

questions.question: Identify the parent function and write an equation for the function shown in the graph.
questions.explanation: The graph is a parabola, so the parent function is $y = x^2$. It opens downward, indicating a reflection across the x-axis, and its vertex is at $(0, 2)$, indicating a vertical shift upward by 2. Therefore, the equation is $f(x) = -x^2 + 2$.
candidate_image_refs: Page 74 bbox [0.15, 0.12, 0.45, 0.28] — Coordinate plane showing a parabola opening downward with its vertex at (0, 2).

questions.question: Identify the parent function and write an equation for the function shown in the graph.
questions.explanation: The graph is a parabola opening upward, so the parent is $y = x^2$. Its vertex is at $(2, -1)$, meaning the graph is shifted 2 units right and 1 unit down. Therefore, the equation is $f(x) = (x - 2)^2 - 1$.
candidate_image_refs: Page 74 bbox [0.55, 0.12, 0.85, 0.28] — Coordinate plane showing a parabola opening upward with its vertex at (2, -1) and passing through the x-axis at x = 1 and x = 3.

questions.question: Identify the parent function and write an equation for the function shown in the graph.
questions.explanation: The graph is V-shaped, so the parent function is $y = |x|$. It opens downward, indicating a reflection across the x-axis, and its vertex is at $(-2, 0)$, indicating a horizontal shift 2 units left. Therefore, the equation is $f(x) = -|x + 2|$.
candidate_image_refs: Page 74 bbox [0.15, 0.32, 0.45, 0.48] — Coordinate plane showing a V-shaped absolute value graph opening downward with its vertex at (-2, 0).

question_types.title: Evaluating Conceptual Properties of Transformations
question_types.mapping_rationale: Evaluating conceptual true-or-false statements about reflections requires a strong grasp of transformation effects on graphs (PC.FBF.3.1) and an understanding of parity and symmetry (PC.FIF.4.2).

questions.question: Determine whether the statement is true or false: The graph of $y = f(-x)$ is a reflection of the graph of $y = f(x)$ in the x-axis.
options: A) False, it is a reflection in the y-axis.
B) True, because the negative sign is inside the function.
C) False, it represents a downward vertical shift.
D) True, because it changes the sign of the output.
questions.correct_option: A) False, it is a reflection in the y-axis.
questions.answer: A) False, it is a reflection in the y-axis.
questions.explanation: Replacing $x$ with $-x$ changes the sign of the input coordinates, which reflects the graph horizontally across the y-axis. A reflection across the x-axis would be represented by $y = -f(x)$.

questions.question: Determine whether the statement is true or false: The graph of $y = -f(x)$ is a reflection of the graph of $y = f(x)$ in the y-axis.
options: A) False, it is a reflection in the x-axis.
B) True, because the negative sign changes the domain.
C) False, it is a reflection through the origin.
D) True, because it multiplies the input by -1.
questions.correct_option: A) False, it is a reflection in the x-axis.
questions.answer: A) False, it is a reflection in the x-axis.
questions.explanation: Multiplying the entire function by $-1$ changes the sign of the output values, which reflects the graph vertically across the x-axis. A reflection in the y-axis is generated by $y = f(-x)$.

questions.question: Determine whether the statement is true or false: The graphs of $f(x) = |x| + 6$ and $f(x) = |-x| + 6$ are identical.
options: A) True, because the absolute value of $x$ is equal to the absolute value of $-x$.
B) False, because the second graph is reflected across the x-axis.
C) False, because the second graph is shifted 6 units to the left.
D) True, because both graphs are shifted 6 units downward.
questions.correct_option: A) True, because the absolute value of $x$ is equal to the absolute value of $-x$.
questions.answer: A) True, because the absolute value of $x$ is equal to the absolute value of $-x$.
questions.explanation: The absolute value function is even, so $|-x| = |x|$ for all real numbers. Replacing $x$ with $-x$ does not change the rule or the graph.

question_types.title: Evaluating Composite Functions Algebraically
question_types.mapping_rationale: Combining functions through composition directly assesses PC.FBF.1.1, while substituting the inner function into the outer function requires manipulating algebraic structure correctly.

questions.question: Find $(f \circ g)(x)$ given $f(x) = x^2$ and $g(x) = x - 1$.
questions.explanation: Compose by substituting $g(x)$ into $f(x)$: $(f \circ g)(x) = f(x - 1) = (x - 1)^2 = x^2 - 2x + 1$.

questions.question: Find $(f \circ g)(x)$ given $f(x) = \sqrt{x+4}$ and $g(x) = x^2$.
questions.explanation: Substitute $g(x) = x^2$ into $f(x)$: $(f \circ g)(x) = f(x^2) = \sqrt{x^2 + 4}$.

questions.question: Find $(f \circ g)(x)$ given $f(x) = \frac{1}{3}x - 3$ and $g(x) = 3x + 1$.
questions.explanation: Substitute $g(x)$ into $f(x)$: $f(3x + 1) = \frac{1}{3}(3x + 1) - 3 = x + \frac{1}{3} - 3 = x - \frac{8}{3}$.

question_types.title: Decomposing Composite Functions
question_types.mapping_rationale: Decomposing a function into f(g(x)) requires interpreting a more complicated expression as nested simpler structures, while also relying on understanding composition rules.

questions.question: Find two functions $f$ and $g$ such that $(f \circ g)(x) = h(x)$, given $h(x) = (2x + 1)^2$.
questions.explanation: The inner function is $g(x) = 2x + 1$, and the outer function squares that result, so $f(x) = x^2$.

questions.question: Find two functions $f$ and $g$ such that $(f \circ g)(x) = h(x)$, given $h(x) = \sqrt[3]{x^2 - 4}$.
questions.explanation: The inner function is $g(x) = x^2 - 4$, and the outer function takes the cube root, so $f(x) = \sqrt[3]{x}$.

questions.question: Find two functions $f$ and $g$ such that $(f \circ g)(x) = h(x)$, given $h(x) = \frac{4}{(5x+2)^2}$.
questions.explanation: The inner function is $g(x) = 5x + 2$, and the outer function maps an input x to $\frac{4}{x^2}$.

question_types.title: Verifying Inverse Functions Algebraically
question_types.mapping_rationale: Verifying inverse relationships by showing that both compositions simplify to x directly aligns with inverse verification and builds on the definition of inverse functions.

questions.question: Show that $f$ and $g$ are inverse functions algebraically: $f(x) = 2x, g(x) = \frac{x}{2}$.
questions.explanation: Compute both compositions: $f(g(x)) = 2\left(\frac{x}{2}\right) = x$ and $g(f(x)) = \frac{2x}{2} = x$.

questions.question: Show that $f$ and $g$ are inverse functions algebraically: $f(x) = x - 5, g(x) = x + 5$.
questions.explanation: Check both compositions: $f(x + 5) = x$ and $g(x - 5) = x$.

questions.question: Show that $f$ and $g$ are inverse functions algebraically: $f(x) = 7x + 1, g(x) = \frac{x-1}{7}$.
questions.explanation: Compute both compositions: $f\left(\frac{x-1}{7}\right) = x$ and $g(7x + 1) = x$.

question_types.title: Finding Inverse Functions Algebraically
question_types.mapping_rationale: Finding an inverse by swapping variables and solving depends on understanding inverse relationships and on skillful algebraic rewriting.

questions.question: Find the inverse function of $f$ algebraically: $f(x) = 2x - 3$.
questions.explanation: Let $y = 2x - 3$, swap x and y to get $x = 2y - 3$, then solve for y: $y = \frac{x+3}{2}$.

questions.question: Find the inverse function of $f$ algebraically: $f(x) = x^5 - 2$.
questions.explanation: Let $y = x^5 - 2$, swap x and y, then solve: $x = y^5 - 2 \Rightarrow x + 2 = y^5 \Rightarrow y = \sqrt[5]{x+2}$.

questions.question: Find the inverse function of $f$ algebraically: $f(x) = x^{3/5}$.
questions.explanation: Let $y = x^{3/5}$, swap x and y to get $x = y^{3/5}$, then raise both sides to the reciprocal power $\frac{5}{3}$: $y = x^{5/3}$.

question_types.title: Verifying Inverses Using the Horizontal Line Test
question_types.mapping_rationale: Using the Horizontal Line Test relies on interpreting graph behavior to determine whether a function is one-to-one, which is the prerequisite for having an inverse function.

questions.question: Use the Horizontal Line Test to determine whether the function has an inverse function. $f(x) = \frac{1}{4}x^3 + 1$
options: A) Yes, because no horizontal line intersects the graph more than once.
B) No, because a horizontal line intersects the graph more than once.
C) Yes, because it passes the Vertical Line Test.
D) No, because it is an odd degree polynomial.
questions.correct_option: A) Yes, because no horizontal line intersects the graph more than once.
questions.answer: A) Yes, because no horizontal line intersects the graph more than once.
questions.explanation: This cubic is strictly increasing, so every horizontal line meets the graph at most once. Therefore the function is one-to-one and has an inverse.

questions.question: Use the Horizontal Line Test to determine whether the function has an inverse function. $h(x) = |x| - 2$
options: A) No, because a horizontal line intersects the graph more than once.
B) Yes, because no horizontal line intersects the graph more than once.
C) No, because it is an absolute value function.
D) Yes, because it is continuous.
questions.correct_option: A) No, because a horizontal line intersects the graph more than once.
questions.answer: A) No, because a horizontal line intersects the graph more than once.
questions.explanation: The V-shaped graph is hit twice by many horizontal lines, so it fails the Horizontal Line Test and does not have an inverse over its full domain.

questions.question: Use the Horizontal Line Test to determine whether the function has an inverse function. $g(x) = \frac{4 - x}{6 x^2}$
options: A) No, because a horizontal line intersects the graph more than once.
B) Yes, because no horizontal line intersects the graph more than once.
C) No, because it has an asymptote at $x=0$.
D) Yes, because it is a rational function.
questions.correct_option: A) No, because a horizontal line intersects the graph more than once.
questions.answer: A) No, because a horizontal line intersects the graph more than once.
questions.explanation: This rational graph is not one-to-one; some horizontal lines cross it at two points, so it fails the Horizontal Line Test.

question_types.title: Performing Polynomial Long Division
question_types.mapping_rationale: Applying the long-division algorithm directly addresses polynomial division and rational-expression rewriting, while aligning and simplifying polynomial terms depends on structural analysis.

questions.question: Divide using long division: $(2x^2 + 10x + 12) \div (x + 3)$
questions.explanation: Divide $2x^2$ by $x$ to get $2x$, subtract, then divide the remaining $4x + 12$ by $x + 3$ to get $4$. The remainder is 0, so the quotient is $2x + 4$.

questions.question: Divide using long division: $(x^3 - 2x^2 - 5x + 6) \div (x - 3)$
questions.explanation: Successive division steps produce quotient terms $x^2$, then $x$, then $-2$, with remainder 0. So the quotient is $x^2 + x - 2$.

questions.question: Divide using long division: $(4x^3 - 7x^2 - 11x + 5) \div (4x + 5)$
questions.explanation: Dividing term by term gives quotient terms $x^2$, $-3x$, and $1$, with remainder 0, so the quotient is $x^2 - 3x + 1$.

question_types.title: Performing Synthetic Division
question_types.mapping_rationale: Synthetic division is a streamlined polynomial-division method that depends on correct coefficient handling, including placeholder zeros for missing powers.

questions.question: Divide using synthetic division: $(3x^3 - 17x^2 + 15x - 25) \div (x - 5)$
questions.explanation: Using synthetic division with 5 and coefficients $[3,-17,15,-25]$ yields quotient coefficients $[3,-2,5]$ and remainder 0.

questions.question: Divide using synthetic division: $(5x^3 + 18x^2 + 7x - 6) \div (x + 3)$
questions.explanation: Using synthetic division with $-3$ gives quotient coefficients $[5,3,-2]$ and remainder 0.

questions.question: Divide using synthetic division: $(x^4 - 10x^2 - 2x + 4) \div (x + 3)$
questions.explanation: Use coefficients $[1,0,-10,-2,4]$ with $-3$. The quotient is $x^3 - 3x^2 - x + 1$ with remainder 1.

question_types.title: Applying the Remainder Theorem
question_types.mapping_rationale: Using synthetic division to evaluate a polynomial at a given value is a direct application of the Remainder Theorem, supported by division mechanics.

questions.question: Use the Remainder Theorem and synthetic division to evaluate the function at $x = 2$: $f(x) = 2x^3 - 7x + 3$.
questions.explanation: Using coefficients $[2,0,-7,3]$ with synthetic value 2 gives remainder 5, so $f(2)=5$.

questions.question: Use the Remainder Theorem and synthetic division to evaluate the function at $x = -2$: $f(x) = 3x^4 - 16x^2 - 4x + 2$.
questions.explanation: Using coefficients $[3,0,-16,-4,2]$ with synthetic value $-2$ gives remainder $-6$, so $f(-2)=-6$.

questions.question: Use the Remainder Theorem and synthetic division to evaluate the function at $x = 3$: $f(x) = x^3 - 4x^2 + 2x - 5$.
questions.explanation: Using coefficients $[1,-4,2,-5]$ with synthetic value 3 gives remainder $-8$, so $f(3)=-8$.

question_types.title: Finding Domain and Asymptotes of Rational Functions
question_types.mapping_rationale: These items focus on rational-function graph features such as asymptotes while also requiring valid-input analysis from denominator restrictions.

questions.question: Find the domain of the function and identify any horizontal and vertical asymptotes: $f(x) = \frac{5x}{x^2 - 9}$
options: C) Domain: All real numbers; VA: None; HA: $y = 0$
questions.explanation: The denominator is zero at $x=\pm3$, giving excluded values and vertical asymptotes. Since the numerator degree is less than the denominator degree, the horizontal asymptote is $y=0$.

questions.question: Find the domain of the function and identify any horizontal and vertical asymptotes: $f(x) = \frac{3x^2}{x^2 - 25}$
options: C) Domain: All real numbers; VA: None; HA: $y = 3$
questions.explanation: The denominator is zero at $x=\pm5$, so those are excluded and produce vertical asymptotes. Equal degrees give horizontal asymptote $y=3$ from leading coefficients.

questions.question: Find the domain of the function and identify any horizontal and vertical asymptotes: $f(x) = \frac{x^2 - 4}{x^2 - 3x - 10}$
questions.explanation: Factoring gives $\frac{(x-2)(x+2)}{(x-5)(x+2)}$, so $x=-2$ is a hole, not a vertical asymptote. The remaining vertical asymptote is at $x=5$, and equal degrees give horizontal asymptote $y=1$.

question_types.title: Finding Intercepts of Rational Functions
question_types.mapping_rationale: These items extract x- and y-intercepts as graph features of rational functions while using algebraic structure to determine valid zeros and function values.

questions.question: Find the x- and y-intercepts of the rational function: $g(x) = \frac{x^2 - 4}{x + 3}$
questions.explanation: Zeros of the numerator give x-intercepts at $x=\pm2$ since the denominator is nonzero there. Substituting $x=0$ gives y-intercept $-\frac{4}{3}$.

questions.question: Find the x- and y-intercepts of the rational function: $h(x) = \frac{x^2 - 9}{x - 1}$
questions.explanation: Setting the numerator equal to zero gives $x=\pm3$ for x-intercepts. Evaluating at $x=0$ gives y-intercept 9.

questions.question: Find the x- and y-intercepts of the rational function: $h(x) = \frac{3x^2 - 5x + 2}{x^2 - 1}$
questions.explanation: Factoring shows a removable factor of $(x-1)$, so $x=1$ is a hole rather than an intercept. The valid x-intercept is at $x=\frac{2}{3}$, and substituting $x=0$ gives y-intercept $-2$.

question_types.title: Identifying Asymptotes of Rational Functions
question_types.mapping_rationale: These items isolate vertical and horizontal asymptotes as key features of rational-function graphs and rely on degree comparisons and denominator analysis.

questions.question: Find all vertical and horizontal asymptotes of the graph of the function: $f(x) = \frac{2x^2 + 1}{x^2 - 1}$
questions.explanation: The denominator is zero at $x=\pm1$, producing vertical asymptotes. Equal degrees give horizontal asymptote equal to the ratio of leading coefficients, $y=2$.

questions.question: Find all vertical and horizontal asymptotes of the graph of the function: $f(x) = \frac{5x^4}{x^2 + 1}$
questions.explanation: The denominator has no real zeros, so there are no vertical asymptotes. The numerator degree exceeds the denominator degree, so there is no horizontal asymptote.

questions.question: Find all vertical and horizontal asymptotes of the graph of the function: $g(x) = \frac{4x}{x^2 + 4}$
questions.explanation: The denominator never equals zero for real x, so there are no vertical asymptotes. Since the numerator degree is less than the denominator degree, the horizontal asymptote is $y=0$.

question_types.title: Solving Polynomial Inequalities
question_types.mapping_rationale: These items use factoring and sign analysis to solve polynomial inequalities, while also interpreting where a graph lies above or below the x-axis.

questions.question: Solve the polynomial inequality and write the solution in interval notation: $x^2 - 3x - 18 \le 0$
questions.explanation: Factor as $(x-6)(x+3)\le0$. The product is nonpositive between the roots, including endpoints, so the solution is $[-3,6]$.

questions.question: Solve the polynomial inequality and write the solution in interval notation: $x^3 - 4x \ge 0$
questions.explanation: Factor as $x(x-2)(x+2)\ge0$. Sign analysis shows the product is nonnegative on $[-2,0]\cup[2,\infty)$.

questions.question: Solve the polynomial inequality and write the solution in interval notation: $2x^2 + 5x > 12$
questions.explanation: Move all terms to one side and factor: $2x^2+5x-12=(2x-3)(x+4)$. The product is positive outside the roots, so the solution is $(-\infty,-4)\cup(\frac{3}{2},\infty)$.

question_types.title: Solving Rational Inequalities
question_types.mapping_rationale: These items combine rational expressions into a single quotient and then use structural sign analysis to solve the resulting inequality.

questions.question: Solve the rational inequality and write the solution in interval notation: $\frac{x+6}{x+1} - 2 < 0$
questions.explanation: Combine into one fraction: $\frac{-x+4}{x+1}<0$. Critical values are $x=-1$ and $x=4$. Sign analysis gives $(-\infty,-1)\cup(4,\infty)$.

questions.question: Solve the rational inequality and write the solution in interval notation: $\frac{2}{x+3} \ge \frac{1}{x-2}$
questions.explanation: Move everything to one side: $\frac{x-7}{(x+3)(x-2)}\ge0$. Excluding $x=-3$ and $x=2$, sign analysis yields $(-3,2)\cup[7,\infty)$.

questions.question: Solve the rational inequality and write the solution in interval notation: $\frac{3x-5}{x-5} \le 4$
questions.explanation: Rewriting gives $\frac{-x+15}{x-5}\le0$. Excluding $x=5$ and using sign analysis gives $(-\infty,5)\cup[15,\infty)$.

question_types.title: Finding the Domain Involving Nonlinear Inequalities
question_types.mapping_rationale: These domain items require setting a radical radicand greater than or equal to zero and solving the resulting nonlinear inequality.

questions.question: Find the domain of the function: $f(x) = \sqrt{x^2 - 9}$
questions.explanation: The radicand must satisfy $x^2-9\ge0$, which factors to $(x-3)(x+3)\ge0$. The solution is outside the roots: $(-\infty,-3]\cup[3,\infty)$.

questions.question: Find the domain of the function: $g(x) = \sqrt{4 - x^2}$
questions.explanation: Require $4-x^2\ge0$. This holds between the roots $-2$ and $2$, inclusive, so the domain is $[-2,2]$.

questions.question: Find the domain of the function: $h(x) = \sqrt{\frac{x}{x-2}}$
questions.explanation: Require $\frac{x}{x-2}\ge0$ with $x\ne2$. Critical values are 0 and 2, and sign analysis yields $(-\infty,0]\cup(2,\infty)$.

question_types.title: Describing Transformations of Exponential Functions
question_types.mapping_rationale: These items identify transformations of exponential parent functions by interpreting how algebraic changes affect graph position and orientation.

questions.question: Describe the transformation from the parent function $f(x) = 5^x$ to $g(x) = 5^{x-2}$.
questions.explanation: Replacing x with $x-2$ produces a horizontal shift 2 units to the right.

questions.question: Describe the transformation from the parent function $f(x) = 4^x$ to $g(x) = 4^x + 1$.
questions.explanation: Adding 1 outside the function produces a vertical shift upward by 1.

questions.question: Describe the transformation from the parent function $f(x) = 3^x$ to $g(x) = 3^{-x}$.
questions.explanation: Replacing x with $-x$ reflects the graph across the y-axis.

question_types.title: Solving Exponential Equations Using the One-to-One Property
question_types.mapping_rationale: These items solve exponential equations by rewriting values with common bases and applying the one-to-one property.

questions.question: Use the One-to-One Property to solve the equation for $x$: $3^{x+1} = 27$.
questions.explanation: Rewrite $27$ as $3^3$, then equate exponents: $x+1=3$, so $x=2$.

questions.question: Use the One-to-One Property to solve the equation for $x$: $2^{x-3} = 16$.
questions.explanation: Rewrite $16$ as $2^4$, then set exponents equal: $x-3=4$, so $x=7$.

questions.question: Use the One-to-One Property to solve the equation for $x$: $2^{x-2} = \frac{1}{32}$.
questions.explanation: Rewrite $\frac{1}{32}$ as $2^{-5}$, then equate exponents: $x-2=-5$, so $x=-3$.

question_types.title: Evaluating Compound Interest Exponential Models
question_types.mapping_rationale: These financial-modeling items apply exponential growth formulas in compound-interest settings and require correct parameter substitution.

questions.question: Find the balance in an account after 10 years if $\$2500$ is invested at an annual rate of $12\%$, compounded annually.
questions.explanation: Use $A=P(1+\frac{r}{n})^{nt}$ with $P=2500$, $r=0.12$, $n=1$, and $t=10$ to get approximately $\$7,764.62$.

questions.question: Find the balance in an account after 10 years if $\$2500$ is invested at an annual rate of $12\%$, compounded continuously.
questions.explanation: Use $A=Pe^{rt}$ with $P=2500$, $r=0.12$, and $t=10$ to get approximately $\$8,300.29$.

questions.question: Find the balance in an account after 40 years if $\$1000$ is invested at an annual rate of $4\%$, compounded daily.
questions.explanation: Use $A=P(1+\frac{r}{n})^{nt}$ with $P=1000$, $r=0.04$, $n=365$, and $t=40$ to get approximately $\$4,952.16$.

question_types.title: Converting Logarithmic Equations to Exponential Form
question_types.mapping_rationale: These items translate between logarithmic and exponential forms by matching base, exponent, and argument roles correctly.

questions.question: Write the logarithmic equation in exponential form: $\log_4 64 = 3$
questions.explanation: Using $\log_b x = y \iff b^y = x$, the equivalent exponential form is $4^3=64$.

questions.question: Write the logarithmic equation in exponential form: $\log_3 81 = 4$
questions.explanation: Using the logarithm definition, the equivalent exponential statement is $3^4=81$.

questions.question: Write the logarithmic equation in exponential form: $\log_{32} 4 = \frac{2}{5}$
questions.explanation: The statement means the base 32 raised to the exponent $\frac{2}{5}$ equals 4.

question_types.title: Evaluating Logarithmic Expressions Algebraically
question_types.mapping_rationale: These items evaluate logarithms without technology by recognizing the exponent needed to produce a given argument from the base.

questions.question: Evaluate the function at the indicated value of $x$ without using a calculator: $f(x) = \log_2 x$, find $f(16)$.
Evaluate the function at the indicated value of $x$ without using a calculator: $f(x) = \log_{16} x$, find $f(4)$.
questions.explanation: Because $16^{1/2}=4$, the value of $\log_{16} 4$ is $\frac{1}{2}$.

questions.question: Evaluate the function at the indicated value of $x$ without using a calculator: $f(x) = \log_7 x$, find $f(1)$.
questions.explanation: Since any nonzero base raised to the power 0 is 1, $\log_7 1=0$.

question_types.title: Solving Logarithmic Equations Using the One-to-One Property
question_types.mapping_rationale: These equations use the one-to-one property of logarithms to equate arguments and solve the resulting algebraic equation.

questions.question: Use the One-to-One Property to solve the equation for $x$: $\log_2(x + 1) = \log_2 4$
questions.explanation: Equal logs with the same base imply equal arguments, so $x+1=4$ and $x=3$.

questions.question: Use the One-to-One Property to solve the equation for $x$: $\log_2(x - 3) = \log_2 9$
questions.explanation: Set arguments equal: $x-3=9$, so $x=12$.

questions.question: Use the One-to-One Property to solve the equation for $x$: $\log(2x + 1) = \log 15$
questions.explanation: Both are common logs, so set arguments equal: $2x+1=15$, which gives $x=7$.

question_types.title: Evaluating Natural Logarithmic Expressions
question_types.mapping_rationale: These items use the inverse relationship between ln and powers of e to simplify natural logarithmic expressions directly.

questions.question: Evaluate the function at the indicated value of $x$ without using a calculator: $f(x) = \ln x$, find $f(e^2)$.
questions.explanation: Since $\ln(e^2)=2$, the value is 2.

questions.question: Evaluate the function at the indicated value of $x$ without using a calculator: $f(x) = \ln x$, find $f(e^{-3})$.
questions.explanation: The inverse property gives $\ln(e^{-3})=-3$.

questions.question: Evaluate the function at the indicated value of $x$ without using a calculator: $f(x) = \ln x$, find $f(e^{-0.32})$.
questions.explanation: Using $\ln(e^a)=a$, the value is $-0.32$.

question_types.title: Applying the Change-of-Base Formula
question_types.mapping_rationale: These items use the change-of-base formula to rewrite logarithms with unfamiliar bases as ratios of more familiar logarithms.

questions.question: Use the change-of-base formula to rewrite the logarithm as a ratio of natural logarithms: $\log_3 17$
questions.explanation: Using $\log_a x = \frac{\ln x}{\ln a}$, we rewrite $\log_3 17$ as $\frac{\ln 17}{\ln 3}$.

questions.question: Use the change-of-base formula to rewrite the logarithm as a ratio of natural logarithms: $\log_\pi 0.5$
questions.explanation: Apply the same formula with base $\pi$ and argument $0.5$ to get $\frac{\ln 0.5}{\ln \pi}$.

questions.question: Use the change-of-base formula to rewrite the logarithm as a ratio of natural logarithms: $\log_{2/3} 0.125$
questions.explanation: Using $\log_a x = \frac{\ln x}{\ln a}$ gives $\frac{\ln 0.125}{\ln(2/3)}$.

question_types.title: Expanding Logarithmic Expressions
question_types.mapping_rationale: These items expand logarithmic expressions using product, quotient, and power properties while preserving algebraic structure.

questions.question: Use the properties of logarithms to expand the expression: $\ln 7x$
questions.explanation: By the product property, $\ln(uv)=\ln u+\ln v$, so $\ln 7x=\ln 7+\ln x$.

questions.question: Use the properties of logarithms to expand the expression: $\log_8 x^4$
questions.explanation: By the power property, $\log_a(u^n)=n\log_a u$, so $\log_8 x^4 = 4\log_8 x$.

questions.question: Use the properties of logarithms to expand the expression: $\ln xyz^2$
questions.explanation: Apply the product property to split factors, then the power property to rewrite $\ln z^2$ as $2\ln z$.

question_types.title: Condensing Logarithmic Expressions
question_types.mapping_rationale: These items reverse log properties to combine multiple terms into a single logarithm and simplify the resulting argument.

questions.question: Condense the expression to the logarithm of a single quantity: $\ln 3 + \ln x$
questions.explanation: Reverse the product property: $\ln u + \ln v = \ln(uv)$, giving $\ln(3x)$.

questions.question: Condense the expression to the logarithm of a single quantity: $\log_3 5x - 4 \log_3 x$
questions.explanation: Rewrite $4\log_3 x$ as $\log_3(x^4)$, then use the quotient property: $\log_3\left(\frac{5x}{x^4}\right)=\log_3\left(\frac{5}{x^3}\right)$.

questions.question: Condense the expression to the logarithm of a single quantity: $\log x + 2 \log(x + 1)$
questions.explanation: Use the power property to rewrite $2\log(x+1)$ as $\log((x+1)^2)$, then combine with the product property.

question_types.title: Solving Exponential Equations Algebraically
question_types.mapping_rationale: These items isolate exponential terms and then use inverse log relationships to solve for the exponent.

questions.question: Solve the exponential equation algebraically: $4(3^x) = 20$
questions.explanation: Divide by 4 to get $3^x=5$, then apply the logarithm definition to obtain $x=\log_3 5$.

questions.question: Solve the exponential equation algebraically: $e^x - 8 = 31$
questions.explanation: Add 8 to get $e^x=39$, then take natural logs to obtain $x=\ln 39$.

questions.question: Solve the exponential equation algebraically: $7 - 2e^x = 5$
questions.explanation: Simplify to $e^x=1$. Since $\ln 1=0$, the solution is $x=0$.

question_types.title: Solving Logarithmic Equations Algebraically
question_types.mapping_rationale: These items isolate logarithms and then exponentiate or convert to exponential form to solve for the variable.

questions.question: Solve the logarithmic equation algebraically: $3 - 4 \ln x = 11$
questions.explanation: Subtract 3 and divide by $-4$ to get $\ln x=-2$, then exponentiate to find $x=e^{-2}$.

questions.question: Solve the logarithmic equation algebraically: $3 + 8 \ln x = 7$
questions.explanation: Subtract 3, divide by 8, and exponentiate: $\ln x=\frac12$, so $x=e^{1/2}$.

questions.question: Solve the logarithmic equation algebraically: $2.1 = \ln 6x$
questions.explanation: Convert to exponential form: $e^{2.1}=6x$, then divide by 6 to solve for x.

question_types.title: Solving Logarithmic Equations Using Properties of Logarithms
question_types.mapping_rationale: These items use log properties and one-to-one reasoning to solve equations, then check domain restrictions to discard extraneous solutions when needed.

questions.question: Solve the logarithmic equation algebraically: $\log(3x + 4) = \log(x - 10)$
questions.explanation: Equating arguments gives $3x+4=x-10$, so $x=-7$. But this makes the original logarithm arguments negative, so there is no real solution.

questions.question: Solve the logarithmic equation algebraically: $\log_2 x + \log_2(x + 2) = \log_2(x + 6)$
questions.explanation: Condense the left side to $\log_2(x(x+2))$, equate arguments, solve the quadratic, then reject $x=-3$ because it is outside the log domain. The valid solution is $x=2$.

questions.question: Solve the logarithmic equation algebraically: $\log_4 x - \log_4(x - 1) = \frac{1}{2}$
questions.explanation: Condense to $\log_4\left(\frac{x}{x-1}\right)=\frac12$, convert to exponential form, and solve to get $x=2$, which satisfies the domain restrictions.

question_types.title: Solving Compound Interest and Financial Models
question_types.mapping_rationale: These items solve for unknown quantities in financial exponential models, including principal and doubling time, using algebraic manipulation and logarithms where needed.

questions.question: Determine the principal $P$ that must be invested at a rate of $r = 5\%$, compounded monthly, so that $\$500,000$ will be available for retirement in $t = 10$ years.
questions.explanation: Use $A=P\left(1+\frac{r}{n}\right)^{nt}$ and solve for $P$ by dividing by the growth factor. This gives approximately $\$303,621.57$.

questions.question: Determine the time necessary for $P$ dollars to double when it is invested at an interest rate of $r = 10\%$ compounded continuously.
questions.explanation: Set $2P=Pe^{0.10t}$, divide by $P$, take natural logs, and solve $t=\frac{\ln2}{0.10}\approx6.93$ years.

questions.question: Determine the time necessary for $P$ dollars to double when it is invested at an interest rate of $r = 6.5\%$ compounded continuously.
questions.explanation: Using continuous compounding, solve $2=e^{0.065t}$ to get $t=\frac{\ln2}{0.065}\approx10.66$ years.

question_types.title: Modeling Exponential Growth and Decay
question_types.mapping_rationale: These items construct and evaluate exponential growth and decay models, including determining a growth constant and using half-life relationships.

questions.question: The number $y$ of hits a new website receives each month can be modeled by $y = 4080e^{kt}$, where $t$ represents the number of months the website has been operating. In the website's third month ($t=3$), there were $10,000$ hits. Find the value of the continuous growth rate $k$.
questions.explanation: Substitute $t=3$ and $y=10000$ into the model, divide by 4080, take natural logs, and solve for $k$ to get $\frac{\ln(125/51)}{3}$.

questions.question: The radioactive isotope $^{226}\text{Ra}$ has a half-life of 1599 years. If the initial quantity is $10\text{ g}$, find the amount remaining after $1000$ years.
questions.explanation: Use the half-life relationship to write the decay model and evaluate after 1000 years. The remaining amount is approximately $6.48\text{ g}$.

questions.question: The radioactive isotope $^{14}\text{C}$ has a half-life of 5715 years. If the initial quantity is $6.5\text{ g}$, find the amount remaining after $1000$ years.
questions.explanation: Apply the half-life decay model with initial amount $6.5\text{ g}$ and time 1000 years. The amount remaining is approximately $5.76\text{ g}$.

question_types.title: Evaluating Logarithmic Scientific Models
question_types.mapping_rationale: These items interpret logarithmic real-world models such as pH, decibels, and the Richter scale by substituting values and evaluating expressions correctly.

questions.question: Use the acidity model $\text{pH} = -\log[\text{H}^+]$, where acidity (pH) is a measure of the hydrogen ion concentration $[\text{H}^+]$ (measured in moles of hydrogen per liter) of a solution. Find the pH when $[\text{H}^+] = 2.3 \times 10^{-5}$.
questions.explanation: Substitute into the model and use log properties: $\text{pH}= -\log(2.3\times10^{-5}) = 5-\log 2.3 \approx 4.64$.

questions.question: The number of decibels $\beta$ of a sound with an intensity of $I$ watts per square meter is given by $\beta = 10 \log(I / I_0)$, where $I_0 = 10^{-12}$ watt per square meter. Find the decibel level of a quiet room where the sound intensity is $I = 10^{-10}$ watt per square meter.
questions.explanation: Compute the intensity ratio $\frac{10^{-10}}{10^{-12}}=10^2$, then $\beta=10\log(10^2)=10\cdot2=20$ decibels.

questions.question: Use the Richter scale $R = \log\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right)$ for measuring the magnitude $R$ of an earthquake. Find the intensity $I$ of an earthquake in Peru in 2015 that measured $R = 7.6$ on the Richter scale. (Let $I_0 = 1$).
questions.explanation: With $I_0=1$, the equation becomes $7.6=\log I$, so converting from log form gives $I=10^{7.6}$.

question_types.title: Finding Coterminal Angles in Radians
question_types.mapping_rationale: These items find coterminal angles by adding or subtracting full rotations of $2\pi$ in radian measure.

questions.question: Determine two coterminal angles (one positive and one negative) for the angle $\frac{\pi}{6}$.
questions.explanation: Add and subtract $2\pi = \frac{12\pi}{6}$: $\frac{\pi}{6}+2\pi=\frac{13\pi}{6}$ and $\frac{\pi}{6}-2\pi=-\frac{11\pi}{6}$.

questions.question: Determine two coterminal angles (one positive and one negative) for the angle $\frac{5\pi}{6}$.
questions.explanation: Add and subtract $2\pi = \frac{12\pi}{6}$ to get $\frac{17\pi}{6}$ and $-\frac{7\pi}{6}$.

questions.question: Determine two coterminal angles (one positive and one negative) for the angle $\frac{2\pi}{3}$.
questions.explanation: Add and subtract $2\pi = \frac{6\pi}{3}$ to get $\frac{8\pi}{3}$ and $-\frac{4\pi}{3}$.

question_types.title: Converting Between Degrees and Radians
question_types.mapping_rationale: These items convert angle measure between degrees and radians using the standard conversion factors.

questions.question: Convert the degree measure to radian measure as a multiple of $\pi$: 120°
questions.explanation: Multiply by $\frac{\pi}{180}$: $120\cdot\frac{\pi}{180}=\frac{2\pi}{3}$.

questions.question: Convert the degree measure to radian measure as a multiple of $\pi$: -20°
questions.explanation: Multiply by $\frac{\pi}{180}$: $-20\cdot\frac{\pi}{180}=-\frac{\pi}{9}$.

questions.question: Convert the radian measure to degree measure: $\frac{3\pi}{2}$
questions.explanation: Multiply by $\frac{180}{\pi}$: $\frac{3\pi}{2}\cdot\frac{180}{\pi}=270°$.

question_types.title: Finding Arc Length and Area of a Circular Sector
question_types.mapping_rationale: These items apply the radian-based formulas for arc length and sector area.

questions.question: Find the length of the arc on a circle of radius $r = 15$ inches intercepted by a central angle $\theta = 120^\circ$.
questions.explanation: Convert $120^\circ$ to $\frac{2\pi}{3}$ radians, then use $s=r\theta=15\cdot\frac{2\pi}{3}=10\pi$.

questions.question: Find the length of the arc on a circle of radius $r = 3$ meters intercepted by a central angle $\theta = 150^\circ$.
questions.explanation: Convert $150^\circ$ to $\frac{5\pi}{6}$ radians, then use $s=r\theta=3\cdot\frac{5\pi}{6}=\frac{5\pi}{2}$.

questions.question: Find the area of the sector of a circle of radius $r = 6$ inches and central angle $\theta = \frac{\pi}{3}$.
questions.explanation: Use $A=\frac12 r^2\theta=\frac12(6)^2\cdot\frac{\pi}{3}=6\pi$ square inches.

question_types.title: Evaluating Sine, Cosine, and Tangent on the Unit Circle
question_types.mapping_rationale: These items evaluate sine, cosine, and tangent from unit-circle coordinates and simplify the resulting exact values.

questions.question: Evaluate the sine, cosine, and tangent of the real number: $t = \frac{\pi}{4}$
questions.explanation: At $\frac{\pi}{4}$, the unit-circle point is $\left(\frac{\sqrt{2}}{2},\frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}\right)$, so sine and cosine are both $\frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}$ and tangent is 1.

questions.question: Evaluate the sine, cosine, and tangent of the real number: $t = -\frac{\pi}{6}$
questions.explanation: At $-\frac{\pi}{6}$, the point is $\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2},-\frac12\right)$, so sine is $-\frac12$, cosine is $\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$, and tangent is $-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{3}$.

questions.question: Evaluate the sine, cosine, and tangent of the real number: $t = -\frac{3\pi}{2}$
options: A) $\sin t = 1, \cos t = 0, \tan t$ is undefined
B) $\sin t = -1, \cos t = 0, \tan t$ is undefined
questions.correct_option: A) $\sin t = 1, \cos t = 0, \tan t$ is undefined
questions.answer: A) $\sin t = 1, \cos t = 0, \tan t$ is undefined
questions.explanation: $-\frac{3\pi}{2}$ is coterminal with $\frac{\pi}{2}$, whose unit-circle point is $(0,1)$, so sine is 1, cosine is 0, and tangent is undefined.

question_types.title: Evaluating All Six Trigonometric Functions
question_types.mapping_rationale: These items extend unit-circle evaluation to all six trig functions by using reciprocal identities along with sine and cosine values.

questions.question: Evaluate (if possible) the six trigonometric functions at the real number $t = \frac{2\pi}{3}$.
options: A) $\sin t = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}, \cos t = -\frac{1}{2}, \tan t = -\sqrt{3}, \csc t = \frac{2\sqrt{3}}{3}, \sec t = -2, \cot t = -\frac{\sqrt{3}}{3}$
B) $\sin t = -\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}, \cos t = -\frac{1}{2}, \tan t = \sqrt{3}, \csc t = -\frac{2\sqrt{3}}{3}, \sec t = -2, \cot t = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{3}$
C) $\sin t = \frac{1}{2}, \cos t = -\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}, \tan t = -\frac{\sqrt{3}}{3}, \csc t = 2, \sec t = -\frac{2\sqrt{3}}{3}, \cot t = -\sqrt{3}$
D) $\sin t = -\frac{1}{2}, \cos t = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}, \tan t = -\frac{\sqrt{3}}{3}, \csc t = -2, \sec t = \frac{2\sqrt{3}}{3}, \cot t = -\sqrt{3}$
questions.correct_option: A) $\sin t = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}, \cos t = -\frac{1}{2}, \tan t = -\sqrt{3}, \csc t = \frac{2\sqrt{3}}{3}, \sec t = -2, \cot t = -\frac{\sqrt{3}}{3}$
questions.answer: A) $\sin t = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}, \cos t = -\frac{1}{2}, \tan t = -\sqrt{3}, \csc t = \frac{2\sqrt{3}}{3}, \sec t = -2, \cot t = -\frac{\sqrt{3}}{3}$
questions.explanation: At $\frac{2\pi}{3}$ the point is $\left(-\frac12,\frac{\sqrt3}{2}\right)$. Use sine = y, cosine = x, tangent = y/x, and reciprocals for csc, sec, and cot.

questions.question: Evaluate (if possible) the six trigonometric functions at the real number $t = \frac{4\pi}{3}$.
options: A) $\sin t = -\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}, \cos t = -\frac{1}{2}, \tan t = \sqrt{3}, \csc t = -\frac{2\sqrt{3}}{3}, \sec t = -2, \cot t = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{3}$
B) $\sin t = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}, \cos t = -\frac{1}{2}, \tan t = -\sqrt{3}, \csc t = \frac{2\sqrt{3}}{3}, \sec t = -2, \cot t = -\frac{\sqrt{3}}{3}$
C) $\sin t = -\frac{1}{2}, \cos t = -\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}, \tan t = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{3}, \csc t = -2, \sec t = -\frac{2\sqrt{3}}{3}, \cot t = \sqrt{3}$
D) $\sin t = -\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}, \cos t = \frac{1}{2}, \tan t = -\sqrt{3}, \csc t = -\frac{2\sqrt{3}}{3}, \sec t = 2, \cot t = -\frac{\sqrt{3}}{3}$
questions.correct_option: A) $\sin t = -\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}, \cos t = -\frac{1}{2}, \tan t = \sqrt{3}, \csc t = -\frac{2\sqrt{3}}{3}, \sec t = -2, \cot t = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{3}$
questions.answer: A) $\sin t = -\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}, \cos t = -\frac{1}{2}, \tan t = \sqrt{3}, \csc t = -\frac{2\sqrt{3}}{3}, \sec t = -2, \cot t = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{3}$
questions.explanation: At $\frac{4\pi}{3}$ the point is $\left(-\frac12,-\frac{\sqrt3}{2}\right)$. Use the coordinate definitions and reciprocal identities.

questions.question: Evaluate (if possible) the six trigonometric functions at the real number $t = -\frac{\pi}{2}$.
options: A) $\sin t = -1, \cos t = 0, \tan t$ is undefined, $\csc t = -1, \sec t$ is undefined, $\cot t = 0$
B) $\sin t = 0, \cos t = -1, \tan t = 0, \csc t$ is undefined, $\sec t = -1, \cot t$ is undefined
C) $\sin t = 1, \cos t = 0, \tan t$ is undefined, $\csc t = 1, \sec t$ is undefined, $\cot t = 0$
D) $\sin t = -1, \cos t = 0, \tan t = 0, \csc t = -1, \sec t = 0, \cot t$ is undefined
questions.correct_option: A) $\sin t = -1, \cos t = 0, \tan t$ is undefined, $\csc t = -1, \sec t$ is undefined, $\cot t = 0$
questions.answer: A) $\sin t = -1, \cos t = 0, \tan t$ is undefined, $\csc t = -1, \sec t$ is undefined, $\cot t = 0$
questions.explanation: At $-\frac{\pi}{2}$ the point is $(0,-1)$. That gives sine $-1$, cosine 0, tangent undefined, cosecant $-1$, secant undefined, and cotangent 0.

question_types.title: Using Even and Odd Trigonometric Properties
question_types.mapping_rationale: These items apply even/odd symmetry of trig functions together with reciprocal identities.

questions.question: Given $\sin t = \frac{1}{2}$, evaluate (a) $\sin(-t)$ and (b) $\csc(-t)$.
questions.explanation: Sine is odd, so $\sin(-t)=-\sin t=-\frac12$. Cosecant is the reciprocal of sine, so $\csc(-t)=-2$.

questions.question: Given $\cos(-t) = -\frac{1}{5}$, evaluate (a) $\cos t$ and (b) $\sec(-t)$.
questions.explanation: Cosine is even, so $\cos t=\cos(-t)=-\frac15$. Secant is the reciprocal of cosine, so $\sec(-t)=-5$.

questions.question: Given $\sin(-t) = \frac{3}{8}$, evaluate (a) $\sin t$ and (b) $\csc t$.
questions.explanation: Since sine is odd, $\sin t=-\frac38$. Then $\csc t$ is the reciprocal, $-\frac83$.

question_types.title: Evaluating Trigonometric Functions Given a Point on the Terminal Side
question_types.mapping_rationale: Evaluating trigonometric functions for a point on the terminal side uses the Cartesian definitions of sine, cosine, and tangent (PC.FT.2.1), together with the Pythagorean relationship to find r (PC.ASE.1.2).

questions.question: The point $(5, 12)$ is on the terminal side of an angle in standard position. Find the exact values of the sine, cosine, and tangent of the angle.
questions.explanation: With $x=5$ and $y=12$, $r=\sqrt{x^2+y^2}=\sqrt{25+144}=13$. Then $\sin\theta=\frac{y}{r}=\frac{12}{13}$, $\cos\theta=\frac{x}{r}=\frac{5}{13}$, and $\tan\theta=\frac{y}{x}=\frac{12}{5}$.

questions.question: The point $(-5, -2)$ is on the terminal side of an angle in standard position. Find the exact values of the sine, cosine, and tangent of the angle.
questions.explanation: Here $x=-5$ and $y=-2$, so $r=\sqrt{25+4}=\sqrt{29}$. Thus $\sin\theta=\frac{-2}{\sqrt{29}}=-\frac{2\sqrt{29}}{29}$, $\cos\theta=\frac{-5}{\sqrt{29}}=-\frac{5\sqrt{29}}{29}$, and $\tan\theta=\frac{-2}{-5}=\frac{2}{5}$.

questions.question: The point $(-4, 10)$ is on the terminal side of an angle in standard position. Find the exact values of the sine, cosine, and tangent of the angle.
questions.explanation: With $x=-4$ and $y=10$, $r=\sqrt{16+100}=\sqrt{116}=2\sqrt{29}$. So $\sin\theta=\frac{10}{2\sqrt{29}}=\frac{5\sqrt{29}}{29}$, $\cos\theta=\frac{-4}{2\sqrt{29}}=-\frac{2\sqrt{29}}{29}$, and $\tan\theta=\frac{10}{-4}=-\frac{5}{2}$.

question_types.title: Determining the Quadrant of an Angle
question_types.mapping_rationale: Determining the quadrant from trigonometric sign information targets quadrant-based behavior of trigonometric functions (PC.FT.2.2), grounded in the coordinate definitions of the functions (PC.FT.2.1).

questions.question: Determine the quadrant in which $\theta$ lies given the following conditions: $\sin \theta > 0$ and $\cos \theta > 0$.
questions.explanation: Sine is positive in Quadrants I and II, while cosine is positive in Quadrants I and IV. The only quadrant where both are positive is Quadrant I.

questions.question: Determine the quadrant in which $\theta$ lies given the following conditions: $\sin \theta < 0$ and $\cos \theta < 0$.
questions.explanation: Sine is negative in Quadrants III and IV, and cosine is negative in Quadrants II and III. The overlap is Quadrant III.

questions.question: Determine the quadrant in which $\theta$ lies given the following conditions: $\csc \theta > 0$ and $\tan \theta < 0$.
questions.explanation: If $\csc\theta>0$, then $\sin\theta>0$, so $\theta$ is in Quadrant I or II. A negative tangent occurs in Quadrant II or IV. The only quadrant satisfying both is Quadrant II.

question_types.title: Evaluating Trigonometric Functions Given a Value and a Constraint
question_types.mapping_rationale: Finding trigonometric values from one value plus a sign or quadrant constraint combines coordinate/ratio definitions of the trig functions (PC.FT.2.1) with quadrant sign reasoning (PC.FT.2.2).

questions.question: Find the exact values of the sine, cosine, and cosecant of $\theta$ given the conditions: $\tan \theta = \frac{15}{8}$ and $\sin \theta > 0$.
questions.explanation: Since $\tan\theta>0$ and $\sin\theta>0$, the angle is in Quadrant I. Let $y=15$ and $x=8$ from $\tan\theta=\frac{15}{8}$. Then $r=\sqrt{8^2+15^2}=17$, so $\sin\theta=\frac{15}{17}$, $\cos\theta=\frac{8}{17}$, and $\csc\theta=\frac{17}{15}$.

questions.question: Find the exact values of the cosine and tangent of $\theta$ given the conditions: $\sin \theta = 0.6$ and $\theta$ lies in Quadrant II.
questions.explanation: Since $\sin\theta=0.6=\frac35$ and the angle is in Quadrant II, take $y=3$, $r=5$, and $x<0$. From $x^2+3^2=5^2$, we get $x=-4$. So $\cos\theta=-\frac45$ and $\tan\theta=-\frac34$.

questions.question: Find the exact values of the sine and cosine of $\theta$ given the conditions: $\cot \theta = -3$ and $\cos \theta > 0$.
questions.explanation: Because $\cot\theta<0$ and $\cos\theta>0$, the angle is in Quadrant IV. Let $x=3$ and $y=-1$ from $\cot\theta=\frac{x}{y}=-3$. Then $r=\sqrt{3^2+(-1)^2}=\sqrt{10}$, so $\sin\theta=-\frac{1}{\sqrt{10}}=-\frac{\sqrt{10}}{10}$ and $\cos\theta=\frac{3}{\sqrt{10}}=\frac{3\sqrt{10}}{10}$.

question_types.title: Finding Reference Angles
question_types.mapping_rationale: Finding a reference angle depends on locating the angle in standard position and determining its acute distance to the x-axis (PC.FT.1.1), with supporting arithmetic in degrees or radians (PC.ASE.1.2).

questions.question: Find the reference angle $\theta'$ for the given angle: $\theta = 160^\circ$.
questions.explanation: Since $160^\circ$ is in Quadrant II, the reference angle is $180^\circ-160^\circ=20^\circ$.

questions.question: Find the reference angle $\theta'$ for the given angle: $\theta = -125^\circ$.
questions.explanation: $-125^\circ$ is coterminal with $235^\circ$, which lies in Quadrant III. The reference angle is $235^\circ-180^\circ=55^\circ$.

questions.question: Find the reference angle $\theta'$ for the given angle: $\theta = \frac{2\pi}{3}$.
questions.explanation: Because $\frac{2\pi}{3}$ is in Quadrant II, its reference angle is $\pi-\frac{2\pi}{3}=\frac{\pi}{3}$.

question_types.title: Finding the Period and Amplitude
question_types.mapping_rationale: Identifying amplitude and period of a sinusoidal function uses key trig graph properties (PC.FT.2.2) by interpreting the parameters in the function rule (PC.ASE.1.2).

questions.question: Find the period and amplitude of the function: $y = -\frac{1}{2} \sin \frac{5x}{4}$
questions.explanation: Amplitude is $|a|=\frac12$. With $b=\frac54$, the period is $\frac{2\pi}{5/4}=\frac{8\pi}{5}$.

questions.question: Find the period and amplitude of the function: $y = -\frac{5}{3} \cos \frac{\pi x}{12}$
questions.explanation: Amplitude is $\left|-\frac53\right|=\frac53$. With $b=\frac{\pi}{12}$, the period is $\frac{2\pi}{\pi/12}=24$.

questions.question: Find the period and amplitude of the function: $y = -\frac{2}{5} \cos 10\pi x$
questions.explanation: Amplitude is $\frac25$. With $b=10\pi$, the period is $\frac{2\pi}{10\pi}=\frac15$.

question_types.title: Describing Transformations of Sine and Cosine
question_types.mapping_rationale: Describing shifts, stretches, and reflections of sine and cosine functions targets function transformations (PC.FBF.3.1) and supports graph interpretation (PC.FIF.7.1).

questions.question: Describe the sequence of transformations from the parent function $f(x) = \cos x$ to $g(x) = \cos(x - \frac{\pi}{2}) + 2$.
options: A) Phase shift right $\frac{\pi}{2}$ units, vertical shift up 2 units
B) Phase shift left $\frac{\pi}{2}$ units, vertical shift up 2 units
C) Phase shift right $\frac{\pi}{2}$ units, vertical shift down 2 units
D) Horizontal shrink by $\frac{1}{2}$, vertical shift up 2 units
questions.correct_option: A) Phase shift right $\frac{\pi}{2}$ units, vertical shift up 2 units
questions.answer: A) Phase shift right $\frac{\pi}{2}$ units, vertical shift up 2 units
questions.explanation: The $(x-\frac\pi2)$ shifts the graph right by $\frac\pi2$, and the $+2$ shifts it up 2.

questions.question: Describe the sequence of transformations from the parent function $f(x) = \cos x$ to $g(x) = 1 + \cos(x + \pi)$.
options: A) Phase shift left $\pi$ units, vertical shift up 1 unit
B) Phase shift right $\pi$ units, vertical shift up 1 unit
C) Phase shift left $\pi$ units, vertical shift down 1 unit
D) Horizontal stretch by $\pi$, vertical shift up 1 unit
questions.correct_option: A) Phase shift left $\pi$ units, vertical shift up 1 unit
questions.answer: A) Phase shift left $\pi$ units, vertical shift up 1 unit
questions.explanation: The $(x+\pi)$ shifts the graph left by $\pi$, and the leading $1+$ shifts it up 1.

questions.question: Describe the sequence of transformations from the parent function $f(x) = \sin x$ to $g(x) = 2 \sin(4x - \pi) - 3$.
options: A) Vertical stretch by 2, horizontal shrink by $\frac{1}{4}$, phase shift right $\frac{\pi}{4}$ units, vertical shift down 3 units
B) Vertical stretch by 2, horizontal shrink by 4, phase shift right $\pi$ units, vertical shift down 3 units
C) Vertical stretch by 2, horizontal stretch by 4, phase shift left $\frac{\pi}{4}$ units, vertical shift down 3 units
D) Amplitude 2, period $4\pi$, phase shift right $\pi$ units, vertical shift down 3 units
questions.correct_option: A) Vertical stretch by 2, horizontal shrink by $\frac{1}{4}$, phase shift right $\frac{\pi}{4}$ units, vertical shift down 3 units
questions.answer: A) Vertical stretch by 2, horizontal shrink by $\frac{1}{4}$, phase shift right $\frac{\pi}{4}$ units, vertical shift down 3 units
questions.explanation: Rewrite as $2\sin(4(x-\frac\pi4))-3$. That gives amplitude 2, horizontal shrink by $\frac14$, right shift $\frac\pi4$, and down 3.

question_types.title: Writing a Trigonometric Equation from Characteristics
question_types.mapping_rationale: Writing sinusoidal equations from specified characteristics applies function-building from transformations (PC.FBF.3.2) and supports periodic modeling (PC.FT.4.1).

questions.question: Write an equation for a sine curve with the following characteristics: Amplitude: $2$, Period: $\pi$, Right phase shift: $\frac{\pi}{2}$, Vertical translation: up $1$ unit.
questions.explanation: Amplitude 2 gives $a=2$. Period $\pi$ gives $b=2$. Right shift $\frac\pi2$ gives $x-\frac\pi2$, and up 1 gives $+1$. So $y=2\sin(2(x-\frac\pi2))+1=2\sin(2x-\pi)+1$.

questions.question: Write an equation for a sine curve with the following characteristics: Amplitude: $3$, Period: $4\pi$, Left phase shift: $\frac{\pi}{4}$, Vertical translation: down $1$ unit.
questions.explanation: Amplitude 3 gives $a=3$. Period $4\pi$ gives $b=\frac12$. Left shift $\frac\pi4$ means $x+\frac\pi4$, and down 1 gives $-1$. Distributing the $\frac12$ gives $y=3\sin(\frac{x}{2}+\frac{\pi}{8})-1$.

questions.question: Write an equation for a cosine curve with the following characteristics: Amplitude: $1$, Period: $\pi$, Left phase shift: $\pi$, Vertical translation: down $\frac{3}{2}$ units.
questions.explanation: Amplitude 1 gives $a=1$. Period $\pi$ gives $b=2$. Left shift $\pi$ gives $x+\pi$, and down $\frac32$ gives $-\frac32$. So $y=\cos(2(x+\pi))-\frac32=\cos(2x+2\pi)-\frac32$.

question_types.title: Modeling Real-World Phenomena with Sine and Cosine
question_types.mapping_rationale: Interpreting periodic real-world models with sine and cosine assesses trigonometric modeling (PC.FT.4.1) and key-feature interpretation such as period (PC.FIF.4.1).

questions.question: The function $P = 100 - 20 \cos\left(\frac{5\pi t}{3}\right)$ approximates the blood pressure $P$ (in millimeters of mercury) at time $t$ (in seconds) for a person at rest. Find the period of the function, which represents the time for one heartbeat.
questions.explanation: Here $b=\frac{5\pi}{3}$, so the period is $\frac{2\pi}{5\pi/3}=\frac65=1.2$ seconds.

questions.question: When tuning a piano, a technician strikes a tuning fork for the A above middle C and sets up a wave motion that can be approximated by $y = 0.001 \sin(880\pi t)$, where $t$ is the time (in seconds). What is the period of the function?
questions.explanation: With $b=880\pi$, the period is $\frac{2\pi}{880\pi}=\frac{1}{440}$ second.

questions.question: The height $h$ (in feet) above ground of a seat on a Ferris wheel at time $t$ (in seconds) is modeled by $h(t) = 53 + 50 \sin\left(\frac{\pi}{10}t - \frac{\pi}{2}\right)$. Find the period of the model, which tells you how long it takes for the Ferris wheel to make one complete revolution.
questions.explanation: Here $b=\frac{\pi}{10}$, so the period is $\frac{2\pi}{\pi/10}=20$ seconds.

question_types.title: Solving Trigonometric Equations in a Specified Interval
question_types.mapping_rationale: Solving trig equations on a bounded interval uses algebraic setup and solving (PC.ASE.2.1) together with unit-circle trig values and angle identification (PC.FT.2.1).

questions.question: Find the solutions of the equation in the interval $[-2\pi, 2\pi]$: $\tan x = 1$
questions.explanation: The reference angle is $\frac\pi4$, and tangent is positive in Quadrants I and III. In $[0,2\pi]$ the solutions are $\frac\pi4$ and $\frac{5\pi}{4}$. Subtracting $2\pi$ gives $-\frac{7\pi}{4}$ and $-\frac{3\pi}{4}$.

questions.question: Find the solutions of the equation in the interval $[-2\pi, 2\pi]$: $\cot x = -\sqrt{3}$
questions.explanation: Since $\cot x=-\sqrt3$, then $\tan x=-\frac{\sqrt3}{3}$. The reference angle is $\frac\pi6$, and tangent is negative in Quadrants II and IV. That gives $\frac{5\pi}{6}$ and $\frac{11\pi}{6}$, plus their values minus $2\pi$.

questions.question: Find the solutions of the equation in the interval $[-2\pi, 2\pi]$: $\sec x = -2$
questions.explanation: Because $\sec x=-2$, we have $\cos x=-\frac12$. The reference angle is $\frac\pi3$, and cosine is negative in Quadrants II and III. So the angles are $\frac{2\pi}{3}$ and $\frac{4\pi}{3}$, plus their values minus $2\pi$.

question_types.title: Verifying Even and Odd Trigonometric Functions
question_types.mapping_rationale: Classifying trigonometric functions as even or odd uses trig symmetry properties (PC.FT.2.3) and substitution-based algebraic reasoning (PC.ASE.1.2).

questions.question: Determine whether the function $f(x) = \sec x$ is even, odd, or neither.
questions.explanation: Since $\cos x$ is even, $\cos(-x)=\cos x$. Therefore $\sec(-x)=\frac{1}{\cos(-x)}=\frac{1}{\cos x}=\sec x$, so secant is even.

questions.question: Determine whether the function $f(x) = x + \tan x$ is even, odd, or neither.
questions.explanation: We have $f(-x)=-x+\tan(-x)=-x-\tan x=-(x+\tan x)=-f(x)$, so the function is odd.

questions.question: Determine whether the function $g(x) = x^2 - \sec x$ is even, odd, or neither.
questions.explanation: Because $(-x)^2=x^2$ and $\sec(-x)=\sec x$, we get $g(-x)=(-x)^2-\sec(-x)=x^2-\sec x=g(x)$, so the function is even.

question_types.title: Analyzing Asymptotic Behavior of Trigonometric Functions
question_types.mapping_rationale: Analyzing asymptotic behavior of tangent, cotangent, and secant emphasizes end behavior and asymptotic features (PC.FIF.7.2) in the context of trigonometric graphs (PC.FT.2.2).

questions.question: Determine the behavior of the function $f(x) = \cot x$ as $x \to 0^+$.
questions.explanation: As $x\to0^+$, $\cos x\to1$ and $\sin x\to0^+$, so $\cot x=\frac{\cos x}{\sin x}\to\infty$.

questions.question: Determine the behavior of the function $f(x) = \tan x$ as $x \to \left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right)^+$.
questions.explanation: As $x\to(\frac\pi2)^+$, $\sin x\to1$ and $\cos x\to0^-$, so $\tan x=\frac{\sin x}{\cos x}\to-\infty$.

questions.question: Determine the behavior of the function $f(x) = \sec x$ as $x \to \left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right)^-$.
questions.explanation: As $x\to(\frac\pi2)^-$, $\cos x\to0^+$, so $\sec x=\frac{1}{\cos x}\to\infty$.

question_types.title: Evaluating Inverse Trigonometric Expressions
question_types.mapping_rationale: Evaluating inverse trigonometric functions requires principal-value reasoning (PC.FT.6.1) and exact-angle interpretation from known trig values (PC.ASE.1.2).

questions.question: Evaluate the exact value of the expression without using a calculator: $\arcsin \frac{1}{2}$
questions.explanation: Arcsin returns the angle in $[-\frac{\pi}{2},\frac{\pi}{2}]$ whose sine matches the input. Since $\sin\frac{\pi}{6}=\frac{1}{2}$, the value is $\frac{\pi}{6}$.

questions.question: Evaluate the exact value of the expression without using a calculator: $\arccos\left(-\frac{1}{2}\right)$
questions.explanation: Arccos returns the angle in $[0,\pi]$ whose cosine matches the input. Since $\cos\frac{2\pi}{3}=-\frac{1}{2}$, the value is $\frac{2\pi}{3}$.

questions.question: Evaluate the exact value of the expression without using a calculator: $\arctan \sqrt{3}$
