questions.explanation: Arctan returns the angle in $(-\frac{\pi}{2},\frac{\pi}{2})$ whose tangent matches the input. Since $\tan\frac{\pi}{3}=\sqrt{3}$, the value is $\frac{\pi}{3}$.

question_types.title: Evaluating Composition of Trigonometric Functions
question_types.mapping_rationale: Evaluating compositions of trig and inverse trig functions applies inverse relationships (PC.FT.6.2) and triangle-based algebraic simplification (PC.ASE.1.2).

questions.question: Find the exact value of the expression without using a calculator: $\sin\left(\arctan \frac{3}{4}\right)$
questions.explanation: Let $\theta=\arctan\frac{3}{4}$, so $\tan\theta=\frac{3}{4}$. A right triangle with opposite 3 and adjacent 4 has hypotenuse 5, so $\sin\theta=\frac{3}{5}$.

questions.question: Find the exact value of the expression without using a calculator: $\cos\left(\arcsin \frac{4}{5}\right)$
questions.explanation: Let $\theta=\arcsin\frac{4}{5}$, so $\sin\theta= rac45$. Using a right triangle with opposite 4 and hypotenuse 5, the adjacent side is 3, so $\cos\theta=\frac{3}{5}$.

questions.question: Find the exact value of the expression: $\sec\left(\arcsin\left(-\frac{3}{5}\right)\right)$
questions.explanation: Let $\theta=\arcsin(-\frac35)$, so $\sin\theta=-\frac35$. In the principal range for arcsin, cosine is nonnegative. Using opposite -3 and hypotenuse 5 gives adjacent 4, so $\cos\theta=\frac45$ and $\sec\theta=\frac54$.

question_types.title: Writing Inverse Trigonometric Expressions Algebraically
question_types.mapping_rationale: Writing algebraic equivalents of inverse-trig compositions emphasizes algebraic structure and simplification (PC.ASE.2.1) within inverse trig contexts (PC.FT.6.2).

questions.question: Write an algebraic expression that is equivalent to the expression: $\cos(\arcsin 2x)$
questions.explanation: Let $\theta=\arcsin 2x$, so $\sin\theta=2x$. Using a triangle with opposite $2x$ and hypotenuse 1, the adjacent side is $\sqrt{1-4x^2}$, so $\cos\theta=\sqrt{1-4x^2}$.

questions.question: Write an algebraic expression that is equivalent to: $\cot(\arctan x)$
questions.explanation: If $\theta=\arctan x$, then $\tan\theta=x$. Cotangent is the reciprocal of tangent, so $\cot\theta=\frac{1}{x}$.

questions.question: Write an algebraic expression that is equivalent to: $\tan(\arccos x)$
questions.explanation: Let $\theta=\arccos x$, so $\cos\theta=x$. Using adjacent $x$ and hypotenuse 1, the opposite side is $\sqrt{1-x^2}$, so $\tan\theta=\frac{\sqrt{1-x^2}}{x}$.

question_types.title: Solving Right Triangles for Missing Measurements
question_types.mapping_rationale: Solving missing measurements in right triangles applies trig modeling (PC.FT.4.1) with supporting algebraic manipulation (PC.ASE.1.2).

questions.question: In a right triangle with acute angle $A = 60^\circ$ and hypotenuse $c = 12$, solve for the length of side $a$.
questions.explanation: Using $\sin A=\frac{a}{c}$, we get $\sin60^\circ=\frac{a}{12}$, so $a=12\cdot\frac{\sqrt3}{2}=6\sqrt3$.

questions.question: Find the length of the shadow cast by a building that is 100 feet tall when the sun is $25^\circ$ above the horizon.
questions.explanation: Let $x$ be the shadow length. Then $\tan25^\circ=\frac{100}{x}$, so $x=\frac{100}{\tan25^\circ}\approx214.45$ feet.
candidate_image_refs: Page 334 bbox [0.08, 0.76, 0.45, 0.91] — A diagram of a building 100 ft tall casting a shadow with the sun at 25 degrees.

question_types.title: Applications Involving Angles of Elevation and Depression
question_types.mapping_rationale: Angles of elevation and depression problems use trig modeling (PC.FT.4.1) and inverse trig expression when solving for angles (PC.FT.6.2).

questions.question: A television camera at ground level films the lift-off of a space shuttle at a point 750 meters from the launch pad. Let $s$ be the height of the shuttle. Write the angle of elevation $\theta$ as a function of $s$.
questions.explanation: Here $\tan\theta=\frac{s}{750}$, so $\theta=\arctan\left(\frac{s}{750}\right)$.
candidate_image_refs: Page 335 bbox [0.08, 0.3, 0.46, 0.45] — A diagram of a camera filming a space shuttle lift-off from 750m away.

questions.question: An observer in a lighthouse 350 feet above sea level observes two ships directly offshore. The angles of depression to the ships are $4^\circ$ and $6.5^\circ$. How far apart are the ships?
questions.explanation: Compute the horizontal distances: $d_1=\frac{350}{\tan6.5^\circ}\approx3070.3$ and $d_2=\frac{350}{\tan4^\circ}\approx4998.6$. Their difference is about $1928.3$ ft.
candidate_image_refs: Page 334 bbox [0.52, 0.59, 0.88, 0.69] — A diagram showing a lighthouse 350 ft tall and two ships with angles of depression of 4 and 6.5 degrees.

question_types.title: Modeling Simple Harmonic Motion
question_types.mapping_rationale: Simple harmonic motion models use trigonometric functions to represent periodic motion (PC.FT.4.1) and rely on amplitude/period properties (PC.FT.2.2).

questions.question: Find a model for simple harmonic motion $d = a \sin \omega t$ or $d = a \cos \omega t$ for a displacement at $t=0$ of $0$ centimeters, an amplitude of $4$ centimeters, and a period of $2$ seconds.
questions.explanation: Starting at 0 means use sine. The amplitude is 4. With period 2, we solve $2=\frac{2\pi}{\omega}$ to get $\omega=\pi$. So the model is $d=4\sin\pi t$.

questions.question: A buoy oscillates in simple harmonic motion as waves go past. The buoy moves a total of 3.5 feet from its low point to its high point and returns to its high point every 10 seconds. Write an equation for the motion of the buoy starting at its high point at $t=0$.
questions.explanation: The amplitude is half the total range, so $a=1.75$. Starting at the high point means use cosine. With period 10, $10=\frac{2\pi}{\omega}$ gives $\omega=\frac{\pi}{5}$, so $d=1.75\cos\left(\frac{\pi}{5}t\right)$.
candidate_image_refs: Page 337 bbox [0.12, 0.17, 0.49, 0.32] — A diagram of a buoy oscillating in simple harmonic motion with waves.

question_types.title: Using Identities to Evaluate a Function
question_types.mapping_rationale: Using trig identities to recover all six trig functions from partial information applies fundamental identities (PC.FT.3.1) with supporting algebraic substitution and simplification (PC.ASE.1.2).

questions.question: Use the given conditions to find the values of all six trigonometric functions: $\sec x = -\frac{5}{2}, \tan x < 0$.
options: A) $\cos x = -\frac{2}{5}, \sin x = \frac{\sqrt{21}}{5}, \tan x = -\frac{\sqrt{21}}{2}, \csc x = \frac{5\sqrt{21}}{21}, \cot x = -\frac{2\sqrt{21}}{21}$
B) $\cos x = -\frac{2}{5}, \sin x = -\frac{\sqrt{21}}{5}, \tan x = \frac{\sqrt{21}}{2}, \csc x = -\frac{5\sqrt{21}}{21}, \cot x = \frac{2\sqrt{21}}{21}$
C) $\cos x = \frac{2}{5}, \sin x = \frac{\sqrt{21}}{5}, \tan x = \frac{\sqrt{21}}{2}, \csc x = \frac{5\sqrt{21}}{21}, \cot x = \frac{2\sqrt{21}}{21}$
D) $\cos x = -\frac{2}{5}, \sin x = \frac{21}{25}, \tan x = -\frac{21}{4}, \csc x = \frac{25}{21}, \cot x = -\frac{4}{21}$
questions.correct_option: A) $\cos x = -\frac{2}{5}, \sin x = \frac{\sqrt{21}}{5}, \tan x = -\frac{\sqrt{21}}{2}, \csc x = \frac{5\sqrt{21}}{21}, \cot x = -\frac{2\sqrt{21}}{21}$
questions.answer: A) $\cos x = -\frac{2}{5}, \sin x = \frac{\sqrt{21}}{5}, \tan x = -\frac{\sqrt{21}}{2}, \csc x = \frac{5\sqrt{21}}{21}, \cot x = -\frac{2\sqrt{21}}{21}$
questions.explanation: Since $\sec x=-\frac52$, we have $\cos x=-\frac25$. With $\tan x<0$, the angle is in Quadrant II, so sine is positive. Then $\sin^2x=1-\cos^2x=1-\frac{4}{25}=\frac{21}{25}$, so $\sin x=\frac{\sqrt{21}}{5}$. The remaining functions follow from quotient and reciprocal identities.

questions.question: Use the given conditions to find the values of all six trigonometric functions: $\tan x = \frac{2}{3}, \cos x > 0$.
options: A) $\sin x = \frac{2\sqrt{13}}{13}, \cos x = \frac{3\sqrt{13}}{13}, \sec x = \frac{\sqrt{13}}{3}, \csc x = \frac{\sqrt{13}}{2}, \cot x = \frac{3}{2}$
B) $\sin x = \frac{3\sqrt{13}}{13}, \cos x = \frac{2\sqrt{13}}{13}, \sec x = \frac{\sqrt{13}}{2}, \csc x = \frac{\sqrt{13}}{3}, \cot x = \frac{3}{2}$
C) $\sin x = -\frac{2\sqrt{13}}{13}, \cos x = -\frac{3\sqrt{13}}{13}, \sec x = -\frac{\sqrt{13}}{3}, \csc x = -\frac{\sqrt{13}}{2}, \cot x = \frac{3}{2}$
D) $\sin x = \frac{2}{13}, \cos x = \frac{3}{13}, \sec x = \frac{13}{3}, \csc x = \frac{13}{2}, \cot x = \frac{3}{2}$
questions.correct_option: A) $\sin x = \frac{2\sqrt{13}}{13}, \cos x = \frac{3\sqrt{13}}{13}, \sec x = \frac{\sqrt{13}}{3}, \csc x = \frac{\sqrt{13}}{2}, \cot x = \frac{3}{2}$
questions.answer: A) $\sin x = \frac{2\sqrt{13}}{13}, \cos x = \frac{3\sqrt{13}}{13}, \sec x = \frac{\sqrt{13}}{3}, \csc x = \frac{\sqrt{13}}{2}, \cot x = \frac{3}{2}$
questions.explanation: Because $\tan x>0$ and $\cos x>0$, the angle is in Quadrant I. Using $1+\tan^2x=\sec^2x$, we get $1+\left(\frac23\right)^2=\frac{13}{9}$, so $\sec x=\frac{\sqrt{13}}{3}$ and $\cos x=\frac{3\sqrt{13}}{13}$. Then $\sin x=\tan x\cdot\cos x=\frac{2\sqrt{13}}{13}$.

question_types.title: Simplifying and Factoring Trigonometric Expressions
question_types.mapping_rationale: Simplifying and factoring trig expressions combines algebraic structure work (PC.ASE.2.1) with use of fundamental trig identities (PC.FT.3.1).

questions.question: Simplify the expression using fundamental identities: $\frac{\sec^2 x - 1}{\sin^2 x}$
questions.explanation: Since $\sec^2x-1=\tan^2x$, the expression becomes $\frac{\tan^2x}{\sin^2x}=\frac{\sin^2x/\cos^2x}{\sin^2x}=\frac{1}{\cos^2x}=\sec^2x$.

questions.question: Factor the expression and use fundamental identities to simplify: $1 - 2\cos^2 x + \cos^4 x$
questions.explanation: This is $(1-\cos^2x)^2$. Since $1-\cos^2x=\sin^2x$, the expression becomes $(\sin^2x)^2=\sin^4x$.

questions.question: Perform the multiplication and use fundamental identities to simplify: $(\sin x + \cos x)^2$
questions.explanation: Expand to get $\sin^2x+2\sin x\cos x+\cos^2x$, then use $\sin^2x+\cos^2x=1$ to obtain $1+2\sin x\cos x$.

question_types.title: Performing Operations with Trigonometric Expressions
question_types.mapping_rationale: Operations on trig expressions emphasize algebraic fraction work (PC.ASE.2.1) supported by fundamental identities (PC.FT.3.1).

questions.question: Add the fractions and simplify: $\frac{1}{1 + \cos x} + \frac{1}{1 - \cos x}$
questions.explanation: Using the common denominator $(1+\cos x)(1-\cos x)=1-\cos^2x=\sin^2x$, the numerator becomes $(1-\cos x)+(1+\cos x)=2$. So the result is $\frac{2}{\sin^2x}=2\csc^2x$.

questions.question: Perform the subtraction and simplify: $\tan x - \frac{\sec^2 x}{\tan x}$
questions.explanation: Combine over the common denominator $\tan x$: $\frac{\tan^2x-\sec^2x}{\tan x}$. Since $\sec^2x=1+\tan^2x$, the numerator is $-1$, so the result is $-\frac{1}{\tan x}=-\cot x$.

question_types.title: Trigonometric Substitution
question_types.mapping_rationale: Trig substitution problems apply Pythagorean identities to expressions after substitution (PC.FT.3.1) with careful algebraic evaluation (PC.ASE.1.2).

questions.question: Use the substitution $x = 3\cos \theta$ to write the algebraic expression $\sqrt{9 - x^2}$ as a trigonometric function of $\theta$, where $0 < \theta < \pi/2$.
questions.explanation: Substitute $x=3\cos\theta$: $\sqrt{9-9\cos^2\theta}=\sqrt{9(1-\cos^2\theta)}=\sqrt{9\sin^2\theta}=3|\sin\theta|$. Since $0<\theta<\frac\pi2$, sine is positive, so the result is $3\sin\theta$.

questions.question: Use the substitution $x = 2\sec \theta$ to write the algebraic expression $\sqrt{x^2 - 4}$ as a trigonometric function of $\theta$, where $0 < \theta < \pi/2$.
questions.explanation: Substitute $x=2\sec\theta$: $\sqrt{4\sec^2\theta-4}=\sqrt{4(\sec^2\theta-1)}=\sqrt{4\tan^2\theta}=2|\tan\theta|$. In the first quadrant, tangent is positive, so the result is $2\tan\theta$.

question_types.title: Verifying Identities by Algebraic Manipulation
question_types.mapping_rationale: Verifying trigonometric identities requires using multiple trig identities (PC.FT.3.2) together with algebraic manipulation such as factoring, expansion, and common denominators (PC.ASE.2.1).

questions.question: Which of the following is the correct first step in verifying the identity $\frac{1 + \sin \theta}{\cos \theta} + \frac{\cos \theta}{1 + \sin \theta} = 2 \sec \theta$?
options: B) Use a Pythagorean identity to replace $1 + \sin \theta$ with $\cos^2 \theta$
C) Distribute the terms to get $\frac{1}{\cos \theta} + \tan \theta + \frac{\cos \theta}{1} + \frac{\cos \theta}{\sin \theta}$
questions.explanation: The first move is combining the two fractions over the common denominator $\cos\theta(1+\sin\theta)$. That produces $\frac{(1+\sin\theta)^2+\cos^2\theta}{\cos\theta(1+\sin\theta)}$, which then simplifies using $\sin^2\theta+\cos^2\theta=1$.

questions.question: Verify the identity by converting the left side to sines and cosines: $\frac{\cot^2 t}{\csc t} = \frac{1 - \sin^2 t}{\sin t}$
options: A) $\frac{\cos^2 t / \sin^2 t}{1 / \sin t} = \frac{\cos^2 t}{\sin t}$
questions.correct_option: A) $\frac{\cos^2 t / \sin^2 t}{1 / \sin t} = \frac{\cos^2 t}{\sin t}$
questions.answer: A) $\frac{\cos^2 t / \sin^2 t}{1 / \sin t} = \frac{\cos^2 t}{\sin t}$
questions.explanation: Replacing $\cot^2 t$ with $\frac{\cos^2 t}{\sin^2 t}$ and $\csc t$ with $\frac{1}{\sin t}$ gives a complex fraction that simplifies to $\frac{\cos^2 t}{\sin t}$. Then use $\cos^2 t = 1 - \sin^2 t$ to match the right-hand side.

questions.question: Which set of identities correctly simplifies $\tan^4 x + \tan^2 x - 3$ into $\sec^2 x(4 \tan^2 x - 3)$?
options: A) Incorrect target; the expression should factor to $(\tan^2 x + 1)(\tan^2 x - 3) = \sec^2 x(\tan^2 x - 3)$
B) Use $1 + \tan^2 x = \sec^2 x$ to replace $\tan^2 x$ everywhere.
C) Factor out $\tan^2 x$ to get $\tan^2 x(\tan^2 x + 1) - 3$.
questions.correct_option: A) Incorrect target; the expression should factor to $(\tan^2 x + 1)(\tan^2 x - 3) = \sec^2 x(\tan^2 x - 3)$
questions.answer: A) Incorrect target; the expression should factor to $(\tan^2 x + 1)(\tan^2 x - 3) = \sec^2 x(\tan^2 x - 3)$
questions.explanation: Treat $\tan^2 x$ as a single variable. Then $\tan^4 x + \tan^2 x - 3$ does not simplify to the stated target. The valid identity form comes from recognizing $\tan^2 x + 1 = \sec^2 x$, giving $(\tan^2 x + 1)(\tan^2 x - 3) = \sec^2 x(\tan^2 x - 3)$.

question_types.title: Verifying Composition of Inverse Trigonometric Functions
question_types.mapping_rationale: Verifying inverse trig compositions uses inverse-function reasoning (PC.FT.6.2) together with triangle-based structural setup and simplification (PC.ASE.1.2).

questions.question: Verify the following identity: $\tan(\sin^{-1} x) = \frac{x}{\sqrt{1 - x^2}}$
options: B) False; the denominator should be $\sqrt{1+x^2}$
C) True; this is a direct application of the quotient identity $\frac{\sin x}{\cos x}$
D) False; the result should be $\sqrt{1-x^2} / x$
questions.explanation: Let $\theta = \sin^{-1}x$, so $\sin\theta = x = \frac{x}{1}$. In a right triangle, opposite is $x$ and hypotenuse is 1, so adjacent is $\sqrt{1-x^2}$. Then $\tan\theta = \frac{x}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}$.

questions.question: Verify the identity: $\cos(\sin^{-1} x) = \sqrt{1 - x^2}$
options: A) True; the adjacent side is $\sqrt{1-x^2}$ and hypotenuse is $1$
B) True; this is simply $\sin^2 x + \cos^2 x = 1$ rewritten
C) False; the result is $1 - x$
D) True; because $\cos x$ and $\sin^{-1} x$ are inverses
questions.correct_option: A) True; the adjacent side is $\sqrt{1-x^2}$ and hypotenuse is $1$
questions.answer: A) True; the adjacent side is $\sqrt{1-x^2}$ and hypotenuse is $1$
questions.explanation: If $\theta = \sin^{-1}x$, then $\sin\theta = x = \frac{x}{1}$. Using a right triangle, the adjacent side is $\sqrt{1-x^2}$, so $\cos\theta = \frac{\sqrt{1-x^2}}{1} = \sqrt{1-x^2}$.

question_types.title: Modeling with Trigonometric Identities
question_types.mapping_rationale: Applying trig identities in real-world models uses trigonometric modeling (PC.FT.4.1) and simplification via identities (PC.FT.3.1).

questions.question: A shadow cast by a vertical gnomon of height $h$ is modeled by $s = \frac{h \sin(90^\circ - \theta)}{\sin \theta}$. Show that this expression is equivalent to $s = h \cot \theta$.
options: A) Use the cofunction identity $\sin(90^\circ - \theta) = \cos \theta$, then use the quotient identity $\cos \theta / \sin \theta = \cot \theta$
B) Use the Pythagorean identity to change $\sin$ to $\cos$
questions.correct_option: A) Use the cofunction identity $\sin(90^\circ - \theta) = \cos \theta$, then use the quotient identity $\cos \theta / \sin \theta = \cot \theta$
questions.answer: A) Use the cofunction identity $\sin(90^\circ - \theta) = \cos \theta$, then use the quotient identity $\cos \theta / \sin \theta = \cot \theta$
questions.explanation: Use the cofunction identity to rewrite $\sin(90^\circ-\theta)$ as $\cos\theta$. Then $s = \frac{h\cos\theta}{\sin\theta} = h\cot\theta$ by the quotient identity.
candidate_image_refs: Page 361 bbox [0.55, 0.22, 0.95, 0.35] — Diagram of a sundial (gnomon) and shadow calculation.

question_types.title: Solving Basic Trigonometric Equations
question_types.mapping_rationale: Solving linear trigonometric equations requires isolating the trigonometric term using standard algebraic operations (PC.ASE.2.1) and then finding the exact angle values using unit circle definitions (PC.FT.2.1).

questions.question: Solve the equation for $x$ in the interval $[0, 2\pi)$: $\sqrt{3} \csc x - 2 = 0$
questions.explanation: First, isolate the cosecant term algebraically: $\sqrt{3} \csc x = 2 \implies \csc x = \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}}$. Using reciprocal identities, convert this to sine: $\sin x = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$. In the interval $[0, 2\pi)$, sine is positive in Quadrants I and II. The reference angle is $\frac{\pi}{3}$. Thus, $x = \frac{\pi}{3}$ and $x = \pi - \frac{\pi}{3} = \frac{2\pi}{3}$.

questions.question: Solve the equation for $x$ in the interval $[0, 2\pi)$: $4 \cos^2 x - 1 = 0$
questions.explanation: Isolate the squared cosine term: $4 \cos^2 x = 1 \implies \cos^2 x = \frac{1}{4}$. Taking the square root of both sides gives $\cos x = \pm \frac{1}{2}$. We must find all angles in $[0, 2\pi)$ where the x-coordinate is $1/2$ or $-1/2$. This occurs at the reference angle $\frac{\pi}{3}$ in all four quadrants: $\frac{\pi}{3}, \frac{2\pi}{3}, \frac{4\pi}{3}, \text{ and } \frac{5\pi}{3}$.

questions.question: Solve the equation for $x$ in the interval $[0, 2\pi)$: $3 \tan^2 x - 1 = 0$
questions.explanation: Isolate the tangent term algebraically: $3 \tan^2 x = 1 \implies \tan^2 x = \frac{1}{3} \implies \tan x = \pm \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} = \pm \frac{\sqrt{3}}{3}$. The reference angle for a tangent of $\frac{\sqrt{3}}{3}$ is $\frac{\pi}{6}$. Because it is $\pm$, the solutions exist in all four quadrants: $\frac{\pi}{6}, \frac{5\pi}{6}, \frac{7\pi}{6}, \text{ and } \frac{11\pi}{6}$.

question_types.title: Solving Quadratic-Type Trigonometric Equations
question_types.mapping_rationale: These equations require treating a trigonometric function as a variable to factor a quadratic expression (PC.ASE.2.1), evaluating the unit circle outcomes of the factored roots (PC.FT.2.1), and sometimes using Pythagorean identities to consolidate mixed trig functions (PC.FT.3.1).

questions.question: Solve the equation for $x$ in the interval $[0, 2\pi)$: $2 \sin^2 x + 3 \sin x + 1 = 0$
questions.explanation:
Treat the equation as a quadratic in terms of $\sin x$ and factor: $(2\sin x + 1)(\sin x + 1) = 0$. 
This yields two possible cases: 
1) $2\sin x + 1 = 0 \implies \sin x = -1/2$. In $[0, 2\pi)$, this occurs at $x = \frac{7\pi}{6}, \frac{11\pi}{6}$. 
2) $\sin x + 1 = 0 \implies \sin x = -1$. In $[0, 2\pi)$, this occurs at $x = \frac{3\pi}{2}$.

questions.question: Solve the equation for $x$ in the interval $[0, 2\pi)$: $\sec^2 x - \sec x = 2$
questions.explanation:
Move all terms to one side to set the quadratic to zero: $\sec^2 x - \sec x - 2 = 0$. Factor the expression: $(\sec x - 2)(\sec x + 1) = 0$. 
Case 1: $\sec x = 2 \implies \cos x = 1/2$. In $[0, 2\pi)$, this gives $x = \frac{\pi}{3}, \frac{5\pi}{3}$. 
Case 2: $\sec x = -1 \implies \cos x = -1$. In $[0, 2\pi)$, this gives $x = \pi$.

questions.question: Solve the equation for $x$ in the interval $[0, 2\pi)$: $2 \sin^2 x = 2 + \cos x$
questions.explanation:
First, use the Pythagorean identity to rewrite the equation in terms of a single trigonometric function: $2(1 - \cos^2 x) = 2 + \cos x$. 
Expand and rearrange into a standard quadratic form: $2 - 2\cos^2 x = 2 + \cos x \implies 2\cos^2 x + \cos x = 0$. 
Factor out the common term: $\cos x(2\cos x + 1) = 0$. 
Case 1: $\cos x = 0 \implies x = \frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{3\pi}{2}$. 
Case 2: $2\cos x + 1 = 0 \implies \cos x = -1/2 \implies x = \frac{2\pi}{3}, \frac{4\pi}{3}$.

question_types.title: Solving Multiple-Angle Trigonometric Equations
question_types.mapping_rationale: Equations with arguments like $2x$ or $3x$ require understanding periodicity to expand the solution interval for the inner argument (PC.FT.2.2) before algebraically isolating the final $x$ values (PC.ASE.2.1).

questions.question: Solve the multiple-angle equation for $x$ in the interval $[0, 2\pi)$: $2 \cos 2x - 1 = 0$
questions.explanation:
Isolate the function algebraically: $2\cos 2x = 1 \implies \cos 2x = 1/2$. 
Since we need solutions for $x \in [0, 2\pi)$, the domain for the argument $2x$ must be evaluated over $[0, 4\pi)$. 
Cosine is $1/2$ at $2x = \frac{\pi}{3}, \frac{5\pi}{3}$ in the first rotation, and $\frac{7\pi}{3}, \frac{11\pi}{3}$ in the second rotation. 
Divide each by 2 to solve for $x$: $\frac{\pi}{6}, \frac{5\pi}{6}, \frac{7\pi}{6}, \frac{11\pi}{6}$.

questions.question: Solve the multiple-angle equation for $x$ in the interval $[0, 2\pi)$: $\tan 3x - 1 = 0$
questions.explanation:
Isolate the tangent function: $\tan 3x = 1$. 
Since $x \in [0, 2\pi)$, the argument $3x$ covers three full rotations: $[0, 6\pi)$. 
Tangent is 1 at $3x = \frac{\pi}{4}, \frac{5\pi}{4}$ (first rotation); $\frac{9\pi}{4}, \frac{13\pi}{4}$ (second); $\frac{17\pi}{4}, \frac{21\pi}{4}$ (third). 
Dividing each value by 3 gives the solutions for $x$: $\frac{\pi}{12}, \frac{5\pi}{12}, \frac{3\pi}{4}, \frac{13\pi}{12}, \frac{17\pi}{12}, \frac{7\pi}{4}$.

questions.question: Solve the multiple-angle equation for $x$ in the interval $[0, 2\pi)$: $\sin \frac{x}{2} = 0$
questions.explanation:
We need to solve $\sin \frac{x}{2} = 0$ for $x \in [0, 2\pi)$. 
This means the inner argument $\frac{x}{2}$ is restricted to the interval $[0, \pi)$. 
In the interval $[0, \pi)$, the sine function is 0 only at $\frac{x}{2} = 0$. 
Multiplying by 2 yields the final solution: $x = 0$. (Note: $\frac{x}{2} = \pi$ is outside the restricted interval for the argument, so $2\pi$ is not included).

question_types.title: Evaluating Exact Values Using Sum and Difference Formulas
question_types.mapping_rationale: Calculating exact values for non-standard angles requires identifying and expanding using sum and difference identities (PC.FT.3.2), fetching the exact unit circle values for standard angles (PC.FT.2.1), and performing precise radical arithmetic to simplify the resulting expressions (PC.ASE.1.2).

questions.question: Find the exact value of $\sin 105^\circ$ by using the fact that $105^\circ = 60^\circ + 45^\circ$.
questions.explanation:
Using the sine sum formula $\sin(u + v) = \sin u \cos v + \cos u \sin v$: 
$\sin(60^\circ + 45^\circ) = \sin 60^\circ \cos 45^\circ + \cos 60^\circ \sin 45^\circ$. 
Substitute the known exact values: $(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2})(\frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}) + (\frac{1}{2})(\frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}) = \frac{\sqrt{6}}{4} + \frac{\sqrt{2}}{4} = \frac{\sqrt{6} + \sqrt{2}}{4}$.

questions.question: Find the exact value of $\cos\left(\frac{\pi}{12}\right)$ given that $\frac{\pi}{12} = \frac{\pi}{3} - \frac{\pi}{4}$.
questions.explanation:
Using the cosine difference formula $\cos(u - v) = \cos u \cos v + \sin u \sin v$: 
$\cos\left(\frac{\pi}{3} - \frac{\pi}{4}\right) = \cos\frac{\pi}{3} \cos\frac{\pi}{4} + \sin\frac{\pi}{3} \sin\frac{\pi}{4}$. 
Substitute exact unit circle values: $(\frac{1}{2})(\frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}) + (\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2})(\frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}) = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{4} + \frac{\sqrt{6}}{4} = \frac{\sqrt{6} + \sqrt{2}}{4}$.

questions.question: Find the exact value of $\tan 15^\circ$ by using the fact that $15^\circ = 60^\circ - 45^\circ$.
questions.explanation:
Using the tangent difference formula $\tan(u - v) = \frac{\tan u - \tan v}{1 + \tan u \tan v}$: 
$\tan(60^\circ - 45^\circ) = \frac{\tan 60^\circ - \tan 45^\circ}{1 + \tan 60^\circ \tan 45^\circ}$. 
Substitute exact values: $\frac{\sqrt{3} - 1}{1 + (\sqrt{3})(1)}$. 
Rationalize the denominator by multiplying by the conjugate $\frac{\sqrt{3} - 1}{\sqrt{3} - 1}$: 
$\frac{(\sqrt{3} - 1)^2}{(\sqrt{3} + 1)(\sqrt{3} - 1)} = \frac{3 - 2\sqrt{3} + 1}{3 - 1} = \frac{4 - 2\sqrt{3}}{2} = 2 - \sqrt{3}$.

question_types.title: Rewriting Trigonometric Expressions Using Formulas
question_types.mapping_rationale: Condensing expanded trigonometric expressions into a single function requires recognizing the structural pattern of the sum and difference formulas backwards (PC.FT.3.2) and correctly managing the operations between the variable/argument terms (PC.ASE.2.1).

questions.question: Write the expression as the sine, cosine, or tangent of a single angle: $\cos \frac{6\pi}{7} \cos \frac{\pi}{7} - \sin \frac{6\pi}{7} \sin \frac{\pi}{7}$
questions.explanation:
The expression matches the structure of the cosine sum formula: $\cos u \cos v - \sin u \sin v = \cos(u + v)$. 
Here, $u = \frac{6\pi}{7}$ and $v = \frac{\pi}{7}$. 
Summing the angles yields $\cos\left(\frac{6\pi}{7} + \frac{\pi}{7}\right) = \cos\left(\frac{7\pi}{7}\right) = \cos \pi$.

questions.question: Write the expression as the sine, cosine, or tangent of a single angle: $\frac{\tan 1.1 + \tan 4.6}{1 - \tan 1.1 \tan 4.6}$
questions.explanation:
This structure precisely matches the tangent sum formula: $\frac{\tan u + \tan v}{1 - \tan u \tan v} = \tan(u + v)$. 
With $u = 1.1$ and $v = 4.6$, condensing the expression results in $\tan(1.1 + 4.6) = \tan 5.7$.

questions.question: Write the expression as the sine, cosine, or tangent of a single angle: $\sin 3 \cos 1.2 - \cos 3 \sin 1.2$
questions.explanation:
The expression matches the sine difference formula: $\sin u \cos v - \cos u \sin v = \sin(u - v)$. 
Here, $u = 3$ and $v = 1.2$. 
Condensing the expression gives $\sin(3 - 1.2) = \sin 1.8$.

question_types.title: Applying Sum and Difference Formulas to Inverse Trig Compositions
question_types.mapping_rationale: Evaluating trigonometric functions where the arguments are sums/differences of inverse functions requires a multi-layered approach: applying composition rules of inverses (PC.FT.6.2), expanding via sum/difference identities (PC.FT.3.2), building right triangles or using Pythagorean identities to find the missing ratio components (PC.FT.2.1), and simplifying the resulting radical algebra (PC.ASE.1.2).

questions.question: Find the exact algebraic value of the trigonometric expression: $\sin(\arcsin x + \arccos x)$
questions.explanation:
Let $u = \arcsin x$ and $v = \arccos x$. 
This means $\sin u = x$ and $\cos v = x$. 
Using right triangles (or the Pythagorean identity $\sin^2\theta + \cos^2\theta = 1$), we find the missing sides: $\cos u = \sqrt{1-x^2}$ and $\sin v = \sqrt{1-x^2}$. 
Apply the sine sum formula: $\sin(u + v) = \sin u \cos v + \cos u \sin v$. 
Substitute the terms: $(x)(x) + (\sqrt{1-x^2})(\sqrt{1-x^2}) = x^2 + (1 - x^2) = 1$.

questions.question: Find the exact algebraic value of the trigonometric expression: $\cos(\arcsin x + \arccos x)$
questions.explanation:
Let $u = \arcsin x$ and $v = \arccos x$. 
From this, $\sin u = x$, $\cos u = \sqrt{1-x^2}$ and $\cos v = x$, $\sin v = \sqrt{1-x^2}$. 
Apply the cosine sum formula: $\cos(u + v) = \cos u \cos v - \sin u \sin v$. 
Substitute the terms: $(\sqrt{1-x^2})(x) - (x)(\sqrt{1-x^2}) = x\sqrt{1-x^2} - x\sqrt{1-x^2} = 0$.

questions.question: Find the exact algebraic value of the trigonometric expression: $\sin(\arctan 2x - \arccos x)$
questions.explanation:
Let $u = \arctan 2x$ and $v = \arccos x$. 
From $u$, we construct a right triangle with opposite $2x$ and adjacent $1$, making the hypotenuse $\sqrt{1+4x^2}$. Thus, $\sin u = \frac{2x}{\sqrt{1+4x^2}}$ and $\cos u = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1+4x^2}}$. 
From $v$, $\cos v = x$ and $\sin v = \sqrt{1-x^2}$. 
Apply the sine difference formula: $\sin(u - v) = \sin u \cos v - \cos u \sin v$. 
Substitute the terms: $\left(\frac{2x}{\sqrt{1+4x^2}}\right)(x) - \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{1+4x^2}}\right)(\sqrt{1-x^2}) = \frac{2x^2 - \sqrt{1-x^2}}{\sqrt{1+4x^2}}$.

question_types.title: Evaluating Trigonometric Functions Involving Double Angles
question_types.mapping_rationale: Evaluating double-angle expressions requires identifying and applying the correct double-angle identity (PC.FT.3.2). Since only partial information is given, students must use quadrant constraints to determine the correct signs of missing trigonometric values (PC.FT.2.2) and correctly simplify the resulting fractions and radicals (PC.ASE.1.2).

questions.question: Use the given conditions to find the exact value of $\tan 2u$: $\sin u = -\frac{3}{5}, \quad \frac{3\pi}{2} < u < 2\pi$
questions.explanation:
Because $u$ is in Quadrant IV, $\cos u$ is positive. $\cos u = \sqrt{1 - (-3/5)^2} = 4/5$. 
This makes $\tan u = \frac{\sin u}{\cos u} = -\frac{3}{4}$. 
Apply the double-angle formula for tangent: $\tan 2u = \frac{2\tan u}{1 - \tan^2 u} = \frac{2(-3/4)}{1 - (-3/4)^2} = \frac{-3/2}{1 - 9/16} = \frac{-3/2}{7/16} = -\frac{24}{7}$.

questions.question: Use the given conditions to find the exact value of $\sin 2u$: $\cos u = -\frac{4}{5}, \quad \frac{\pi}{2} < u < \pi$
questions.explanation:
Because $u$ is in Quadrant II, $\sin u$ is positive. $\sin u = \sqrt{1 - (-4/5)^2} = \sqrt{1 - 16/25} = \frac{3}{5}$. 
Apply the double-angle formula for sine: $\sin 2u = 2\sin u \cos u = 2\left(\frac{3}{5}\right)\left(-\frac{4}{5}\right) = -\frac{24}{25}$.

questions.question: Use the given conditions to find the exact value of $\cos 2u$: $\tan u = \frac{3}{5}, \quad 0 < u < \frac{\pi}{2}$
questions.explanation:
In Quadrant I, both sine and cosine are positive. A right triangle with opposite side 3 and adjacent side 5 has a hypotenuse of $r = \sqrt{3^2 + 5^2} = \sqrt{34}$. 
Therefore, $\sin u = \frac{3}{\sqrt{34}}$ and $\cos u = \frac{5}{\sqrt{34}}$. 
Using the double-angle formula for cosine: $\cos 2u = \cos^2 u - \sin^2 u = \left(\frac{5}{\sqrt{34}}\right)^2 - \left(\frac{3}{\sqrt{34}}\right)^2 = \frac{25}{34} - \frac{9}{34} = \frac{16}{34} = \frac{8}{17}$.

question_types.title: Evaluating Trigonometric Functions Involving Half Angles
question_types.mapping_rationale: Applying half-angle identities (PC.FT.3.2) requires a critical intermediate step: determining the correct sign of the radical by identifying the quadrant of the half-angle, not the original angle (PC.FT.2.2). The final step involves simplifying nested radicals or complex fractions (PC.ASE.1.2).

questions.question: Use the half-angle formulas to determine the exact value of $\sin 165^\circ$.
questions.explanation:
Rewrite $165^\circ$ as $\frac{330^\circ}{2}$. Since $165^\circ$ is in Quadrant II, its sine is positive. 
Using the half-angle formula for sine: $\sin\left(\frac{u}{2}\right) = +\sqrt{\frac{1 - \cos u}{2}}$. 
Substitute $u = 330^\circ$: $\sqrt{\frac{1 - \cos 330^\circ}{2}} = \sqrt{\frac{1 - \sqrt{3}/2}{2}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 - \sqrt{3}}{4}} = \frac{\sqrt{2 - \sqrt{3}}}{2}$.

questions.question: Use the given conditions to find the exact value of $\sin\left(\frac{u}{2}\right)$: $\cos u = \frac{7}{25}, \quad 0 < u < \frac{\pi}{2}$
questions.explanation:
Since $0 < u < \pi/2$, dividing by 2 gives $0 < \frac{u}{2} < \pi/4$. Thus, the half-angle is in Quadrant I, where sine is positive. 
Using the half-angle formula: $\sin\left(\frac{u}{2}\right) = +\sqrt{\frac{1 - \cos u}{2}}$. 
Substitute the value of $\cos u$: $\sqrt{\frac{1 - 7/25}{2}} = \sqrt{\frac{18/25}{2}} = \sqrt{\frac{9}{25}} = \frac{3}{5}$.

questions.question: Use the given conditions to find the exact value of $\cos\left(\frac{u}{2}\right)$: $\sin u = \frac{5}{13}, \quad \frac{\pi}{2} < u < \pi$
questions.explanation:
Because $u$ is in Quadrant II, $\cos u$ is negative: $\cos u = -\sqrt{1 - (5/13)^2} = -\frac{12}{13}$. 
The half-angle range is $\frac{\pi}{4} < \frac{u}{2} < \frac{\pi}{2}$, which is in Quadrant I, so $\cos\left(\frac{u}{2}\right)$ is positive. 
Apply the half-angle formula: $\cos\left(\frac{u}{2}\right) = +\sqrt{\frac{1 + \cos u}{2}} = \sqrt{\frac{1 - 12/13}{2}} = \sqrt{\frac{1/13}{2}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{26}} = \frac{\sqrt{26}}{26}$.

question_types.title: Solving Trigonometric Equations using Multiple-Angle Formulas
question_types.mapping_rationale: Solving these equations requires algebraic restructuring and factoring (PC.ASE.2.1) after applying double or half-angle identities to consolidate the arguments to a single variable (PC.FT.3.2). Final solutions are determined by mapping the roots to standard unit circle values (PC.FT.2.1).

questions.question: Find all solutions of the equation in the interval $[0, 2\pi)$: $\sin 2x - \sin x = 0$
questions.explanation:
Substitute using the double-angle formula for sine: $2\sin x \cos x - \sin x = 0$. 
Factor out the common term: $\sin x(2\cos x - 1) = 0$. 
Set each factor to zero: 
1) $\sin x = 0 \implies x = 0, \pi$. 
2) $2\cos x - 1 = 0 \implies \cos x = 1/2 \implies x = \frac{\pi}{3}, \frac{5\pi}{3}$.

questions.question: Find all solutions of the equation in the interval $[0, 2\pi)$: $\cos 2x - \cos x = 0$
questions.explanation:
Use the double-angle formula for cosine in terms of cosine only: $(2\cos^2 x - 1) - \cos x = 0$. 
Rearrange into standard quadratic form: $2\cos^2 x - \cos x - 1 = 0$. 
Factor the quadratic: $(2\cos x + 1)(\cos x - 1) = 0$. 
Set each factor to zero: 
1) $\cos x = -1/2 \implies x = \frac{2\pi}{3}, \frac{4\pi}{3}$. 
2) $\cos x = 1 \implies x = 0$.

questions.question: Find all solutions of the equation in the interval $[0, 2\pi)$: $\sin\left(\frac{x}{2}\right) + \cos x = 0$
questions.explanation:
Use the half-angle substitution or the double-angle formula applied inversely: $\cos x = 1 - 2\sin^2\left(\frac{x}{2}\right)$. 
Substitute into the equation: $\sin\left(\frac{x}{2}\right) + 1 - 2\sin^2\left(\frac{x}{2}\right) = 0 \implies 2\sin^2\left(\frac{x}{2}\right) - \sin\left(\frac{x}{2}\right) - 1 = 0$. 
Factor the quadratic: $\left(2\sin\left(\frac{x}{2}\right) + 1\right)\left(\sin\left(\frac{x}{2}\right) - 1\right) = 0$. 
1) $\sin\left(\frac{x}{2}\right) = 1 \implies \frac{x}{2} = \frac{\pi}{2} \implies x = \pi$. 
2) $\sin\left(\frac{x}{2}\right) = -1/2 \implies \frac{x}{2} = \frac{7\pi}{6}, \frac{11\pi}{6} \implies x = \frac{7\pi}{3}, \frac{11\pi}{3}$. 
Since we are restricted to $[0, 2\pi)$, $\frac{7\pi}{3}$ and $\frac{11\pi}{3}$ are outside the domain. The only valid solution is $\pi$.

question_types.title: Solving Oblique Triangles (AAS or ASA)
question_types.mapping_rationale: Solving triangles given Angle-Angle-Side (AAS) or Angle-Side-Angle (ASA) conditions requires direct application of the Law of Sines (PC.FT.4.2). Setting up the proportions and isolating the unknown variable requires algebraic manipulation (PC.ASE.2.1) and numerical evaluation (PC.ASE.1.2).

questions.question: Use the Law of Sines to solve the triangle. Round your answers to two decimal places: $A = 102.4^\circ, C = 16.7^\circ, a = 21.6$.
questions.explanation: First, find the third angle: $B = 180^\circ - 102.4^\circ - 16.7^\circ = 60.9^\circ$. Next, use the Law of Sines to find $c$: $\frac{c}{\sin 16.7^\circ} = \frac{21.6}{\sin 102.4^\circ} \implies c = \frac{21.6 \sin 16.7^\circ}{\sin 102.4^\circ} \approx 6.36$. Finally, find $b$: $\frac{b}{\sin 60.9^\circ} = \frac{21.6}{\sin 102.4^\circ} \implies b = \frac{21.6 \sin 60.9^\circ}{\sin 102.4^\circ} \approx 19.32$.

questions.question: Use the Law of Sines to solve the triangle. Round your answers to two decimal places: $A = 35^\circ, B = 65^\circ, c = 10$.
questions.explanation: Find angle $C$: $C = 180^\circ - 35^\circ - 65^\circ = 80^\circ$. Apply the Law of Sines for $a$: $\frac{a}{\sin 35^\circ} = \frac{10}{\sin 80^\circ} \implies a = \frac{10 \sin 35^\circ}{\sin 80^\circ} \approx 5.82$. Apply the Law of Sines for $b$: $\frac{b}{\sin 65^\circ} = \frac{10}{\sin 80^\circ} \implies b = \frac{10 \sin 65^\circ}{\sin 80^\circ} \approx 9.20$.

questions.question: Use the Law of Sines to solve the triangle. Round your answers to two decimal places: $A = 24.3^\circ, C = 54.6^\circ, c = 2.68$.
questions.explanation: Calculate the missing angle: $B = 180^\circ - 24.3^\circ - 54.6^\circ = 101.1^\circ$. For side $a$: $a = \frac{2.68 \sin 24.3^\circ}{\sin 54.6^\circ} \approx 1.35$. For side $b$: $b = \frac{2.68 \sin 101.1^\circ}{\sin 54.6^\circ} \approx 3.23$.

question_types.title: The Ambiguous Case (SSA)
question_types.mapping_rationale: The ambiguous case (Side-Side-Angle) requires students to apply the Law of Sines (PC.FT.4.2), evaluate the resulting equations (PC.ASE.2.1), and use unit circle inverse reasoning to recognize the possibility of an acute and an obtuse angle solution (PC.FT.2.1).

questions.question: Use the Law of Sines to solve the triangle (if possible). If two solutions exist, find both. Round your answers to two decimal places: $A = 110^\circ, a = 125, b = 100$.
options: B) Two solutions: $B_1 \approx 48.74^\circ, C_1 \approx 21.26^\circ, c_1 \approx 48.23$ AND $B_2 \approx 131.26^\circ, C_2 \approx -61.26^\circ, c_2 \approx 48.23$
questions.explanation: Since angle $A$ is obtuse and $a > b$ ($125 > 100$), there is exactly one solution. Find $B$: $\sin B = \frac{100 \sin 110^\circ}{125} \approx 0.7517 \implies B \approx 48.74^\circ$. The third angle is $C = 180^\circ - 110^\circ - 48.74^\circ = 21.26^\circ$. Finally, $c = \frac{125 \sin 21.26^\circ}{\sin 110^\circ} \approx 48.23$.

questions.question: Use the Law of Sines to solve the triangle (if possible). If two solutions exist, find both. Round your answers to two decimal places: $A = 76^\circ, a = 18, b = 20$.
options: C) Two solutions: $B_1 \approx 74.2^\circ, C_1 \approx 29.8^\circ, c_1 \approx 9.2$ AND $B_2 \approx 105.8^\circ, C_2 \approx -1.8^\circ, c_2 \approx 1.1$
questions.explanation: To check for solutions, find the height of the triangle: $h = b \sin A = 20 \sin 76^\circ \approx 19.4$. Since side $a$ (18) is less than the height $h$ (19.4), the side is too short to reach the base, meaning no triangle can be formed.

questions.question: Use the Law of Sines to solve the triangle (if possible). If two solutions exist, find both. Round your answers to two decimal places: $A = 58^\circ, a = 11.4, b = 12.8$.
options: A) Two solutions: $B_1 \approx 72.21^\circ, C_1 \approx 49.79^\circ, c_1 \approx 10.27$ AND $B_2 \approx 107.79^\circ, C_2 \approx 14.21^\circ, c_2 \approx 3.30$
D) Two solutions: $B_1 \approx 65.12^\circ, C_1 \approx 56.88^\circ, c_1 \approx 11.25$ AND $B_2 \approx 114.88^\circ, C_2 \approx 7.12^\circ, c_2 \approx 1.66$
questions.correct_option: A) Two solutions: $B_1 \approx 72.21^\circ, C_1 \approx 49.79^\circ, c_1 \approx 10.27$ AND $B_2 \approx 107.79^\circ, C_2 \approx 14.21^\circ, c_2 \approx 3.30$
questions.answer: A) Two solutions: $B_1 \approx 72.21^\circ, C_1 \approx 49.79^\circ, c_1 \approx 10.27$ AND $B_2 \approx 107.79^\circ, C_2 \approx 14.21^\circ, c_2 \approx 3.30$
questions.explanation:
Find the height: $h = 12.8 \sin 58^\circ \approx 10.85$. Because $h < a < b$ ($10.85 < 11.4 < 12.8$), there are two valid triangles. 
For triangle 1: $\sin B = \frac{12.8 \sin 58^\circ}{11.4} \approx 0.9522 \implies B_1 \approx 72.21^\circ$. Then $C_1 = 180^\circ - 58^\circ - 72.21^\circ = 49.79^\circ$, and $c_1 = \frac{11.4 \sin 49.79^\circ}{\sin 58^\circ} \approx 10.27$.
For triangle 2 (obtuse B): $B_2 = 180^\circ - 72.21^\circ = 107.79^\circ$. Then $C_2 = 180^\circ - 58^\circ - 107.79^\circ = 14.21^\circ$, and $c_2 = \frac{11.4 \sin 14.21^\circ}{\sin 58^\circ} \approx 3.30$.

question_types.title: Finding the Area of an Oblique Triangle
question_types.mapping_rationale: Calculating the area of a non-right triangle applies trigonometric relationships to geometric figures (PC.FT.4.2) using the formula Area = 0.5 * a * b * sin(C). Correct execution requires accurate algebraic/numerical evaluation (PC.ASE.1.2).

questions.question: Find the area of the triangle having the indicated angle and sides. Round your answer to two decimal places: $A = 125^\circ, b = 9, c = 6$.
questions.explanation:
Use the trigonometric area formula: $\text{Area} = \frac{1}{2}bc \sin A$. 
Substitute the given values: $\text{Area} = \frac{1}{2}(9)(6) \sin 125^\circ = 27 \sin 125^\circ \approx 22.12$.

questions.question: Find the area of the triangle having the indicated angle and sides. Round your answer to two decimal places: $B = 39^\circ, a = 25, c = 12$.
questions.explanation:
Use the area formula: $\text{Area} = \frac{1}{2}ac \sin B$. 
Substitute the values: $\text{Area} = \frac{1}{2}(25)(12) \sin 39^\circ = 150 \sin 39^\circ \approx 94.40$.

questions.question: Find the area of the triangle having the indicated angle and sides. Round your answer to two decimal places: $C = 103^\circ 15', a = 16, b = 28$.
questions.explanation:
First, convert the angle to decimal degrees: $103^\circ 15' = 103.25^\circ$. 
Use the formula: $\text{Area} = \frac{1}{2}ab \sin C = \frac{1}{2}(16)(28) \sin 103.25^\circ = 224 \sin 103.25^\circ \approx 218.04$.

question_types.title: Applications of the Law of Sines
question_types.mapping_rationale: Solving word problems with bearings and distances involves modeling real-world scenarios with trigonometric tools (PC.FT.4.1), executing the Law of Sines accurately (PC.FT.4.2), and computing the final algebraic proportions (PC.ASE.1.2).

questions.question: The bearing from Pine Knob fire tower to Colt Station fire tower is $N \ 65^\circ \ E$, and the two towers are 30 kilometers apart. A fire is spotted by rangers in each tower. The bearing from Pine Knob to the fire is $N \ 80^\circ \ E$. The bearing from Colt Station to the fire is $S \ 70^\circ \ E$. Find the distance of the fire from each tower.
options: A) Pine Knob: $42.4$ km; Colt Station: $15.5$ km
B) Pine Knob: $15.5$ km; Colt Station: $42.4$ km
C) Pine Knob: $36.2$ km; Colt Station: $18.1$ km
D) Pine Knob: $28.9$ km; Colt Station: $12.4$ km
questions.correct_option: A) Pine Knob: $42.4$ km; Colt Station: $15.5$ km
questions.answer: A) Pine Knob: $42.4$ km; Colt Station: $15.5$ km
questions.explanation:
Let A = Pine Knob, B = Colt Station, and F = Fire. 
Angle at A: The fire is $80^\circ$ from North, and B is $65^\circ$ from North. The inner angle $\angle FAB = 80^\circ - 65^\circ = 15^\circ$. 
Angle at B: Using parallel North-South lines, the bearing from B to A is $S \ 65^\circ \ W$. The bearing from B to F is $S \ 70^\circ \ E$. The total angle $\angle ABF = 65^\circ + 70^\circ = 135^\circ$.
Angle at F: $\angle AFB = 180^\circ - 15^\circ - 135^\circ = 30^\circ$.
Use Law of Sines for Pine Knob to Fire ($b$): $\frac{b}{\sin 135^\circ} = \frac{30}{\sin 30^\circ} \implies b = \frac{30(\sqrt{2}/2)}{0.5} \approx 42.43$ km.
Use Law of Sines for Colt Station to Fire ($a$): $\frac{a}{\sin 15^\circ} = \frac{30}{\sin 30^\circ} \implies a = 60 \sin 15^\circ \approx 15.53$ km.
candidate_image_refs: Page 407 bbox [0.15, 0.78, 0.49, 0.92] — A diagram mapping Pine Knob, Colt Station, and the fire using bearings.

questions.question: A bridge is built across a small lake from a gazebo to a dock. The bearing from the gazebo to the dock is $S \ 41^\circ \ W$. From a tree 100 meters from the gazebo, the bearings to the gazebo and the dock are $S \ 74^\circ \ E$ and $S \ 28^\circ \ E$, respectively. Find the distance from the gazebo to the dock.
questions.explanation:
Let T = Tree, G = Gazebo, D = Dock. We know $TG = 100$. 
At the tree (T), the angle between G and D is $\angle GTD = 74^\circ - 28^\circ = 46^\circ$. 
At the gazebo (G), the bearing to D is $S \ 41^\circ \ W$. The bearing to T is the reverse of $S \ 74^\circ \ E$, which is $N \ 74^\circ \ W$. The angle $\angle TGD$ spans the West side of the North-South axis: $180^\circ - 74^\circ - 41^\circ = 65^\circ$. 
The third angle at the dock (D) is $\angle TDG = 180^\circ - 46^\circ - 65^\circ = 69^\circ$. 
To find the distance from gazebo to dock ($GD$), use the Law of Sines: $\frac{GD}{\sin 46^\circ} = \frac{100}{\sin 69^\circ} \implies GD = \frac{100 \sin 46^\circ}{\sin 69^\circ} \approx 77.1$ meters.
candidate_image_refs: Page 407 bbox [0.55, 0.17, 0.94, 0.32] — A diagram showing the lake, gazebo, dock, and tree with their respective bearings.

question_types.title: Solving Oblique Triangles (SSS and SAS)
question_types.mapping_rationale: Solving triangles given Side-Side-Side (SSS) or Side-Angle-Side (SAS) requires the direct application of the Law of Cosines (PC.FT.4.2). To find missing angles, students must algebraically isolate the cosine term (PC.ASE.2.1) and apply inverse trigonometric functions (PC.FT.6.1), followed by precise numerical evaluation (PC.ASE.1.2).

questions.question: Use the Law of Cosines to solve the triangle. Round your answers to two decimal places: $A = 30^\circ, b = 12, c = 24$.
questions.explanation: Use the Law of Cosines to find side $a$: $a^2 = b^2 + c^2 - 2bc \cos A = 12^2 + 24^2 - 2(12)(24)\cos 30^\circ = 144 + 576 - 576(\sqrt{3}/2) = 720 - 288\sqrt{3} \approx 221.17$. Taking the square root gives $a \approx 14.87$. Next, use the Law of Sines to find the smaller missing angle $B$: $\frac{\sin B}{12} = \frac{\sin 30^\circ}{14.87} \implies \sin B \approx 0.4035 \implies B \approx 23.79^\circ$. The third angle is $C = 180^\circ - 30^\circ - 23.79^\circ = 126.21^\circ$.

questions.question: Use the Law of Cosines to solve the triangle. Round your answers to two decimal places: $a = 9, b = 12, c = 15$.
questions.explanation: Because $9^2 + 12^2 = 81 + 144 = 225 = 15^2$, this is a right triangle (a 3-4-5 multiple), so $C = 90^\circ$. Using the Law of Cosines (or basic right triangle trig): $\cos A = \frac{b^2 + c^2 - a^2}{2bc} = \frac{144 + 225 - 81}{2(12)(15)} = \frac{288}{360} = 0.8$. Thus, $A = \arccos(0.8) \approx 36.87^\circ$. Finally, $B = 180^\circ - 90^\circ - 36.87^\circ = 53.13^\circ$.

questions.question: Use the Law of Cosines to solve the triangle. Round your answers to two decimal places: $a = 1.42, b = 0.75, c = 1.25$.
questions.explanation: Find the largest angle ($A$) first to avoid the ambiguous case later: $\cos A = \frac{b^2 + c^2 - a^2}{2bc} = \frac{0.75^2 + 1.25^2 - 1.42^2}{2(0.75)(1.25)} \approx \frac{0.5625 + 1.5625 - 2.0164}{1.875} = \frac{0.1086}{1.875} \approx 0.05792$. Therefore, $A = \arccos(0.05792) \approx 86.68^\circ$. Find $B$: $\cos B = \frac{a^2 + c^2 - b^2}{2ac} \approx \frac{2.0164 + 1.5625 - 0.5625}{3.55} = \frac{3.0164}{3.55} \approx 0.8497 \implies B \approx 31.82^\circ$. Finally, $C = 180^\circ - 86.68^\circ - 31.82^\circ = 61.50^\circ$.

question_types.title: Using Heron's Area Formula
question_types.mapping_rationale: Calculating the area of a triangle given three sides requires the application of Heron's Formula (PC.FT.4.2). The execution of this formula heavily relies on multi-step arithmetic, calculating the semi-perimeter, and accurately evaluating nested square roots containing decimals (PC.ASE.1.2).

questions.question: Use Heron's Area Formula to find the area of the triangle. Round your answer to two decimal places: $a = 5, b = 9, c = 10$.
questions.explanation:
First, find the semi-perimeter $s = \frac{a+b+c}{2} = \frac{5+9+10}{2} = 12$. 
Heron's Formula is $\text{Area} = \sqrt{s(s-a)(s-b)(s-c)}$. 
Substitute the values: $\text{Area} = \sqrt{12(12-5)(12-9)(12-10)} = \sqrt{12(7)(3)(2)} = \sqrt{504} \approx 22.45$.

questions.question: Use Heron's Area Formula to find the area of the triangle. Round your answer to two decimal places: $a = 3.5, b = 10.2, c = 9$.
questions.explanation:
Calculate the semi-perimeter $s = \frac{3.5+10.2+9}{2} = \frac{22.7}{2} = 11.35$. 
Apply Heron's Formula: $\text{Area} = \sqrt{11.35(11.35-3.5)(11.35-10.2)(11.35-9)}$. 
$\text{Area} = \sqrt{11.35(7.85)(1.15)(2.35)} \approx \sqrt{240.82} \approx 15.52$.

questions.question: Use Heron's Area Formula to find the area of the triangle. Round your answer to two decimal places: $a = 10.59, b = 6.65, c = 12.31$.

question_types.title: Applications of the Law of Cosines
question_types.mapping_rationale: Solving real-world navigational and geometric word problems requires modeling physical distances and angles (PC.FT.4.1). Translating these models into the Law of Cosines (PC.FT.4.2) and calculating the final scalar values (PC.ASE.1.2) are critical secondary skills.

questions.question: Two ships leave a port at 9 A.M. One travels at a bearing of $N \ 53^\circ \ W$ at 12 miles per hour, and the other travels at a bearing of $S \ 67^\circ \ W$ at 16 miles per hour. Approximate how far apart they are at noon that day.
questions.explanation:
First, calculate the distance each ship traveled in 3 hours (9 A.M. to noon). Ship A: $3 \cdot 12 = 36$ miles. Ship B: $3 \cdot 16 = 48$ miles. 
Next, find the angle between their paths. A straight North-South line is $180^\circ$. Since both ships are traveling West, the angle between them is $180^\circ - 53^\circ - 67^\circ = 60^\circ$. 
Apply the Law of Cosines to find the distance $d$ between them: $d^2 = 36^2 + 48^2 - 2(36)(48)\cos 60^\circ = 1296 + 2304 - 3456(0.5) = 3600 - 1728 = 1872$. 
$d = \sqrt{1872} \approx 43.27$ miles.

questions.question: A plane flies 810 miles from Franklin to Centerville with a bearing of $75^\circ$. Then it flies 648 miles from Centerville to Rosemount with a bearing of $32^\circ$. Draw a figure that visually represents the situation, and find the straight-line distance and bearing from Franklin to Rosemount.
questions.explanation:
Let F = Franklin, C = Centerville, R = Rosemount. 
The flight from F to C is along a bearing of $75^\circ$ (from North). At C, the back-bearing to F is $180^\circ + 75^\circ = 255^\circ$. The new bearing to R is $32^\circ$. 
The interior angle at C is the difference between the back-bearing and the new bearing: $255^\circ - 32^\circ = 223^\circ$. The inner triangle angle is $360^\circ - 223^\circ = 137^\circ$. (Alternatively, $180^\circ - 75^\circ + 32^\circ = 137^\circ$). 
Use the Law of Cosines to find distance $d$: $d^2 = 810^2 + 648^2 - 2(810)(648)\cos 137^\circ$. 
$d^2 = 656100 + 419904 - 1049760(-0.73135) \approx 1076004 + 767746 = 1843750$. 
$d = \sqrt{1843750} \approx 1357.8$ miles.

questions.question: A triangular parcel of land has sides of lengths 115 meters, 76 meters, and 92 meters. To the nearest tenth of a degree, what is the measure of the largest angle?
questions.explanation:
The largest angle is always opposite the longest side. Here, the longest side is $115$ meters. 
Using the Law of Cosines solved for the angle: $\cos A = \frac{b^2 + c^2 - a^2}{2bc}$. 
Substitute the values: $\cos A = \frac{76^2 + 92^2 - 115^2}{2(76)(92)} = \frac{5776 + 8464 - 13225}{13984} = \frac{1015}{13984} \approx 0.07258$. 
Take the inverse cosine: $A = \arccos(0.07258) \approx 85.8^\circ$.

question_types.title: Solving Linear Systems by Substitution
question_types.unit_label: Chapter 7: Systems of Equations and Inequalities
question_types.mapping_rationale: Solving systems of two linear equations relies directly on the substitution method algorithm (PC.AREI.6.1), while manipulating the expressions and managing fractions requires foundational algebraic skills (PC.ASE.2.1).

questions.question:
Solve the system of equations by the method of substitution: 
$\begin{cases} x - y = 2 \\ 6x - 5y = 16 \end{cases}$
questions.explanation: Solve the first equation for $x$: $x = y + 2$. Substitute this into the second equation: $6(y + 2) - 5y = 16 \implies 6y + 12 - 5y = 16 \implies y = 4$. Substitute $y = 4$ back into $x = y + 2$ to find $x = 6$. The solution is $(6, 4)$.

questions.question:
Solve the system of equations by the method of substitution: 
$\begin{cases} \frac{1}{5}x + \frac{1}{2}y = 8 \\ x + y = 20 \end{cases}$
questions.explanation: Solve the second equation for $y$: $y = 20 - x$. Substitute into the first equation: $\frac{1}{5}x + \frac{1}{2}(20 - x) = 8$. Distribute: $\frac{1}{5}x + 10 - \frac{1}{2}x = 8$. Combine like terms: $\left(\frac{2}{10} - \frac{5}{10}\right)x = -2 \implies -\frac{3}{10}x = -2 \implies x = \frac{20}{3}$. Finally, $y = 20 - \frac{20}{3} = \frac{40}{3}$.

question_types.title: Solving Nonlinear Systems by Substitution
question_types.unit_label: Chapter 7: Systems of Equations and Inequalities
question_types.mapping_rationale: Solving systems containing both linear and nonlinear (quadratic, circle) equations targets PC.AREI.7.1. Factoring the resulting quadratics and correctly identifying multiple intersection points requires strong algebraic manipulation (PC.ASE.2.1).

questions.question:
Solve the system of equations by the method of substitution: 
$\begin{cases} x - y = -4 \\ x^2 - y = -2 \end{cases}$
questions.explanation: Solve the first equation for $y$: $y = x + 4$. Substitute this into the second equation: $x^2 - (x + 4) = -2 \implies x^2 - x - 4 = -2 \implies x^2 - x - 2 = 0$. Factor the quadratic: $(x - 2)(x + 1) = 0$. This yields $x = 2$ and $x = -1$. Substitute back to find $y$: if $x = 2$, $y = 6$; if $x = -1$, $y = 3$.
candidate_image_refs: Page 475 bbox [0.15, 0.67, 0.31, 0.77] — A coordinate plane showing a parabola intersecting a straight line at two points.

questions.question:
Solve the system of equations by the method of substitution: 
$\begin{cases} -\frac{1}{2}x + y = -\frac{5}{2} \\ x^2 + y^2 = 25 \end{cases}$
questions.explanation:
Solve the first equation for $y$: $y = \frac{1}{2}x - \frac{5}{2}$. Substitute into the circle equation: $x^2 + (\frac{1}{2}x - \frac{5}{2})^2 = 25$. Expand: $x^2 + \frac{1}{4}x^2 - \frac{5}{2}x + \frac{25}{4} = 25$. Multiply the entire equation by 4 to clear fractions: $4x^2 + x^2 - 10x + 25 = 100 \implies 5x^2 - 10x - 75 = 0$. Divide by 5: $x^2 - 2x - 15 = 0 \implies (x - 5)(x + 3) = 0$. 
If $x = 5$, $y = \frac{1}{2}(5) - \frac{5}{2} = 0$. 
If $x = -3$, $y = \frac{1}{2}(-3) - \frac{5}{2} = -4$.
candidate_image_refs: Page 475 bbox [0.77, 0.35, 0.94, 0.46] — A coordinate plane showing a circle intersected by a straight line at two points.

questions.question:
Solve the system of equations by the method of substitution: 
$\begin{cases} x^2 - y = 0 \\ 2x + y = 0 \end{cases}$
questions.explanation:
From the first equation, $y = x^2$. Substitute this into the second equation: $2x + x^2 = 0$. Factor out an $x$: $x(x + 2) = 0$. This yields $x = 0$ and $x = -2$. 
If $x = 0$, $y = 0^2 = 0$. 
If $x = -2$, $y = (-2)^2 = 4$. 
The solutions are $(0, 0)$ and $(-2, 4)$.

question_types.title: Applications of Systems of Equations
question_types.unit_label: Chapter 7: Systems of Equations and Inequalities
question_types.mapping_rationale: These real-world scenarios require translating text into a mathematical system (PC.CED.2.1), then using substitution to find the intersection/break-even point (PC.AREI.7.1), often necessitating careful handling of large numbers or geometric polynomials (PC.ASE.1.2).

questions.question:
Use the equations for the total cost $C$ and total revenue $R$ to find the number $x$ of units a company must sell to break even (where $R = C$). Round to the nearest whole unit. 
$C = 8650x + 250,000$ 
$R = 9502x$
questions.explanation:
To break even, set $R = C$. 
$9502x = 8650x + 250,000$ 
Subtract $8650x$ from both sides: $852x = 250,000$. 
Divide by 852: $x = \frac{250000}{852} \approx 293.42$. 
Rounded to the nearest whole unit, the company must sell $293$ units.

questions.question:
Use a system of equations to find the dimensions of the rectangle meeting the specified conditions: 
The perimeter is 56 meters and the length is 4 meters greater than the width.
questions.explanation:
Let $l$ be the length and $w$ be the width. The system is: 
$2l + 2w = 56$ (Perimeter) 
$l = w + 4$ (Length condition) 
Substitute the second equation into the first: $2(w + 4) + 2w = 56 \implies 2w + 8 + 2w = 56 \implies 4w = 48 \implies w = 12$. 
Since $l = w + 4$, $l = 12 + 4 = 16$. The dimensions are $12$ m by $16$ m.

questions.question:
Use a system of equations to find the dimensions of the rectangle meeting the specified conditions: 
What are the dimensions of a rectangular tract of land when its perimeter is 44 kilometers and its area is 120 square kilometers?
questions.explanation:
Set up the nonlinear system for length $l$ and width $w$: 
$2l + 2w = 44 \implies l + w = 22 \implies l = 22 - w$ 
$l \cdot w = 120$ 
Substitute $l$: $(22 - w)w = 120 \implies 22w - w^2 = 120 \implies w^2 - 22w + 120 = 0$. 
Factor the quadratic: $(w - 10)(w - 12) = 0$. 
The dimensions are $10$ km by $12$ km.

question_types.title: Solving Linear Systems by Elimination
question_types.unit_label: Chapter 7: Systems of Equations and Inequalities
question_types.mapping_rationale: Applying the elimination method to solve a two-variable linear system directly targets PC.AREI.6.1, while the necessary steps of scalar multiplication and algebraic addition draw on foundational expression manipulation (PC.ASE.2.1).

questions.question:
Solve the system by the method of elimination: 
$\begin{cases} x + 2y = 6 \\ x - 2y = 2 \end{cases}$
questions.explanation: Add the two equations together to eliminate $y$: $(x + 2y) + (x - 2y) = 6 + 2 \implies 2x = 8 \implies x = 4$. Substitute $x = 4$ into the first equation: $4 + 2y = 6 \implies 2y = 2 \implies y = 1$. The solution is $(4, 1)$.

questions.question:
Solve the system by the method of elimination: 
$\begin{cases} 5x + 3y = 6 \\ 3x - y = 5 \end{cases}$
questions.explanation: Multiply the second equation by 3 to get $9x - 3y = 15$. Add this to the first equation: $(5x + 3y) + (9x - 3y) = 6 + 15 \implies 14x = 21 \implies x = \frac{21}{14} = \frac{3}{2}$. Substitute $x = 1.5$ into the second equation: $3(1.5) - y = 5 \implies 4.5 - y = 5 \implies y = -0.5$. The solution is $(\frac{3}{2}, -\frac{1}{2})$.

question_types.title: Identifying Inconsistent and Dependent Systems
question_types.unit_label: Chapter 7: Systems of Equations and Inequalities
question_types.mapping_rationale: Recognizing when a system has no solution or infinitely many solutions requires executing the elimination or substitution method (PC.AREI.6.1) and interpreting the resulting degenerate algebraic statements like 0 = -10 (PC.ASE.2.1).

questions.question:
Solve the system by the method of elimination. Describe the number of solutions: 
$\begin{cases} 7x - 6y = -6 \\ -7x + 6y = -4 \end{cases}$
questions.explanation: Adding the two equations directly yields $0 = -10$. Since this is a false statement, the lines are parallel and never intersect, meaning there is no solution.

questions.question:
Find the value of $k$ such that the system of linear equations is inconsistent: 
$\begin{cases} 4x - 8y = -3 \\ 2x + ky = 16 \end{cases}$
questions.explanation: For a linear system to be inconsistent, the lines must be parallel, meaning their slopes must be equal but their y-intercepts must be different. Setting the ratio of the $x$-coefficients equal to the ratio of the $y$-coefficients: $\frac{4}{2} = \frac{-8}{k} \implies 2 = \frac{-8}{k} \implies 2k = -8 \implies k = -4$.

question_types.title: Applications of Two-Variable Linear Systems
question_types.unit_label: Chapter 7: Systems of Equations and Inequalities
question_types.mapping_rationale: Translating real-world scenarios (like speed, distance, or chemical mixtures) into a system of equations evaluates modeling skills (PC.CED.2.1). Solving that system relies on linear methods (PC.AREI.6.1) and accurate numeric/decimal arithmetic (PC.ASE.1.2).

questions.question: An airplane flying into a headwind travels the 1800-mile flying distance between Indianapolis, Indiana, and Phoenix, Arizona, in 3 hours. On the return flight, the airplane travels this distance in 2 hours and 30 minutes. Find the airspeed of the plane and the speed of the wind, assuming that both remain constant.
options: A) Airspeed: $660$ mph, Wind speed: $60$ mph
B) Airspeed: $700$ mph, Wind speed: $50$ mph
C) Airspeed: $600$ mph, Wind speed: $120$ mph
D) Airspeed: $720$ mph, Wind speed: $80$ mph
questions.correct_option: A) Airspeed: $660$ mph, Wind speed: $60$ mph
questions.answer: A) Airspeed: $660$ mph, Wind speed: $60$ mph
questions.explanation:
Let $r$ be the airspeed and $w$ be the wind speed. Using Distance = Rate $\times$ Time:
Headwind: $1800 = (r - w) \cdot 3 \implies r - w = 600$
Tailwind (2.5 hrs): $1800 = (r + w) \cdot 2.5 \implies r + w = \frac{1800}{2.5} = 720$
Adding the two simplified equations: $2r = 1320 \implies r = 660$ mph. 
Substituting back: $660 + w = 720 \implies w = 60$ mph.

questions.question: Thirty liters of a 40% acid solution is obtained by mixing a 25% solution with a 50% solution. Write and solve a system of equations to find how much of each solution is required.
options: A) $12$ liters of 25% solution, $18$ liters of 50% solution
B) $15$ liters of 25% solution, $15$ liters of 50% solution
C) $18$ liters of 25% solution, $12$ liters of 50% solution
D) $10$ liters of 25% solution, $20$ liters of 50% solution
questions.correct_option: A) $12$ liters of 25% solution, $18$ liters of 50% solution
questions.answer: A) $12$ liters of 25% solution, $18$ liters of 50% solution
questions.explanation:
Let $x$ be the amount of 25% solution and $y$ be the amount of 50% solution.
Total volume: $x + y = 30$
Total acid: $0.25x + 0.50y = 0.40(30) \implies 0.25x + 0.50y = 12$
Multiply the first equation by $-0.25$: $-0.25x - 0.25y = -7.5$
Add this to the second equation: $0.25y = 4.5 \implies y = 18$ liters.
Substitute back into volume equation: $x + 18 = 30 \implies x = 12$ liters.

question_types.title: Vector Operations and Unit Vectors
question_types.mapping_rationale: Calculating vector sums, differences, and scalar multiples addresses fundamental vector operations (PC.VM.1.1). Finding a unit vector requires dividing by the magnitude, which heavily relies on radical arithmetic and rationalization (PC.ASE.1.2).

questions.question: Find the vector $2\mathbf{u} - 3\mathbf{v}$ given $\mathbf{u} = \langle 2, 1 \rangle$ and $\mathbf{v} = \langle 1, 3 \rangle$.
questions.explanation: Multiply each vector by its scalar: $2\langle 2, 1 \rangle = \langle 4, 2 \rangle$ and $3\langle 1, 3 \rangle = \langle 3, 9 \rangle$. Then subtract the corresponding components: $\langle 4 - 3, 2 - 9 \rangle = \langle 1, -7 \rangle$.

questions.question: Find a unit vector in the direction of the given vector: $\mathbf{v} = \langle -2, 2 \rangle$.
questions.explanation: First, find the magnitude of the vector: $||\mathbf{v}|| = \sqrt{(-2)^2 + 2^2} = \sqrt{4 + 4} = \sqrt{8} = 2\sqrt{2}$. Then, divide each component of the original vector by the magnitude: $\langle \frac{-2}{2\sqrt{2}}, \frac{2}{2\sqrt{2}} \rangle = \langle -\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}, \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \rangle$. Rationalizing the denominator yields $\langle -\frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}, \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} \rangle$.

questions.question: Write the vector as a linear combination of the standard unit vectors $\mathbf{i}$ and $\mathbf{j}$, given its initial point $(-2, 1)$ and terminal point $(3, -2)$.
questions.explanation: To find the component form, subtract the initial coordinates from the terminal coordinates: $\langle 3 - (-2), -2 - 1 \rangle = \langle 5, -3 \rangle$. Writing this as a linear combination of the standard unit vectors gives $5\mathbf{i} - 3\mathbf{j}$.

question_types.title: Vector Components and Direction Angles
question_types.mapping_rationale: Converting between a vector's component form and its magnitude/direction angle (PC.VM.1.2) heavily utilizes right triangle trigonometry and unit circle values (PC.FT.2.1), along with radical arithmetic (PC.ASE.1.2).

questions.question: Find the magnitude and direction angle of the vector: $\mathbf{v} = 6\mathbf{i} - 6\mathbf{j}$.
questions.explanation: Magnitude is $||\mathbf{v}|| = \sqrt{6^2 + (-6)^2} = \sqrt{72} = 6\sqrt{2}$. The direction angle satisfies $\tan \theta = \frac{-6}{6} = -1$. Because the vector is in Quadrant IV (positive x, negative y), the correct direction angle is $315^\circ$.

questions.question: Find the component form of $\mathbf{v}$ given its magnitude $||\mathbf{v}|| = \frac{7}{2}$ and the angle it makes with the positive x-axis is $\theta = 150^\circ$.
questions.explanation: Use the formula $\mathbf{v} = ||\mathbf{v}|| \langle \cos\theta, \sin\theta \rangle$. Substituting the given values: $\mathbf{v} = \frac{7}{2} \langle \cos 150^\circ, \sin 150^\circ \rangle$. Using unit circle values, this becomes $\frac{7}{2} \langle -\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}, \frac{1}{2} \rangle$. Distributing the scalar yields $\langle -\frac{7\sqrt{3}}{4}, \frac{7}{4} \rangle$.

questions.question: Find the component form of the sum of $\mathbf{u}$ and $\mathbf{v}$ with the given magnitudes and direction angles: $||\mathbf{u}|| = 4, \theta_u = 60^\circ$; $||\mathbf{v}|| = 4, \theta_v = 90^\circ$.
questions.explanation:
First, find the component form for each vector. 
$\mathbf{u} = 4\langle \cos 60^\circ, \sin 60^\circ \rangle = 4\langle \frac{1}{2}, \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \rangle = \langle 2, 2\sqrt{3} \rangle$. 
$\mathbf{v} = 4\langle \cos 90^\circ, \sin 90^\circ \rangle = 4\langle 0, 1 \rangle = \langle 0, 4 \rangle$. 
Adding the components together: $\mathbf{u} + \mathbf{v} = \langle 2 + 0, 2\sqrt{3} + 4 \rangle = \langle 2, 4 + 2\sqrt{3} \rangle$.

question_types.title: Vector Applications: Force and Tension
question_types.mapping_rationale: Solving statics and dynamics word problems requires modeling physical forces as vectors (PC.VM.2.1), setting up trigonometric equations based on right triangle or oblique triangle geometry (PC.FT.4.1), and occasionally utilizing the Law of Cosines to find resulting angles (PC.FT.4.2).

questions.question: A force of $F$ pounds is required to pull an object weighing $W$ pounds up a ramp inclined at $\theta$ degrees from the horizontal. Find $F$ when $W = 100$ pounds and $\theta = 12^\circ$.
questions.explanation: To maintain equilibrium, the pulling force $F$ must be equal to the component of the object's weight that is directed parallel to the ramp. This component is given by $W \sin\theta$. Calculating this yields $100 \sin(12^\circ) \approx 20.79$ pounds.

questions.question: A tetherball weighing 1 pound is pulled outward from the pole by a horizontal force $\mathbf{u}$ until the rope makes a $45^\circ$ angle with the pole. Determine the resulting tension in the rope and the magnitude of the horizontal force $\mathbf{u}$.
questions.explanation: In static equilibrium, the vertical component of the tension $T$ must balance the weight: $T \cos 45^\circ = 1 \implies T = 1 / \cos 45^\circ = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.41$ lbs. The horizontal component of the tension balances the outward force $\mathbf{u}$: $||\mathbf{u}|| = T \sin 45^\circ = \sqrt{2} \sin 45^\circ = 1$ lb.
candidate_image_refs: Page 427 bbox [0.85, 0.15, 0.98, 0.40] — A diagram of a tetherball pulled by a horizontal force u, making a 45 degree angle with the pole.

questions.question: Find the angle between two forces given the magnitude of their resultant. Force 1 is 45 pounds, Force 2 is 60 pounds, and the Resultant Force is 90 pounds.
questions.explanation:
When two vectors are added, they form a parallelogram. The resultant is the diagonal. Using the Law of Cosines for the vector sum: $R^2 = F_1^2 + F_2^2 + 2F_1 F_2 \cos\theta$, where $\theta$ is the angle between them. 
$90^2 = 45^2 + 60^2 + 2(45)(60)\cos\theta$ 
$8100 = 2025 + 3600 + 5400\cos\theta$ 
$2475 = 5400\cos\theta \implies \cos\theta = \frac{2475}{5400} \approx 0.4583$. 
$\theta = \arccos(0.4583) \approx 62.7^\circ$.

question_types.title: Finding the Dot Product of Two Vectors
question_types.mapping_rationale: Calculating the dot product requires applying the specific vector arithmetic definition (PC.VM.1.1) and executing the subsequent algebraic multiplication and addition accurately (PC.ASE.1.2).

questions.question: Find the dot product $\mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{v}$ given $\mathbf{u} = \langle 7, 1 angle$ and $\mathbf{v} = \langle -3, 2 angle$.
questions.explanation: The dot product is the sum of the products of the corresponding components: $\mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{v} = (7)(-3) + (1)(2) = -21 + 2 = -19$.

questions.question: Find the dot product $\mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{v}$ given $\mathbf{u} = \langle -6, 2 angle$ and $\mathbf{v} = \langle 1, 3 angle$.
questions.explanation: Multiply the corresponding components and add them together: $\mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{v} = (-6)(1) + (2)(3) = -6 + 6 = 0$.

questions.question: Find the dot product $\mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{v}$ given $\mathbf{u} = 4\mathbf{i} - 2\mathbf{j}$ and $\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{i} - \mathbf{j}$.
questions.explanation: Extract the coefficients for $\mathbf{i}$ and $\mathbf{j}$, then calculate the dot product: $\mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{v} = (4)(1) + (-2)(-1) = 4 + 2 = 6$.

question_types.title: Finding the Angle Between Two Vectors
question_types.mapping_rationale: Calculating the angle between vectors requires relating component forms to geometric direction via the dot product formula (PC.VM.1.2). It then requires using inverse trigonometric functions to solve for the specific angle (PC.FT.6.1) and applying complex radical arithmetic to find the magnitudes (PC.ASE.1.2).

questions.question: Find the angle $	heta$ (in radians) between the vectors: $\mathbf{u} = \langle 1, 0 angle$ and $\mathbf{v} = \langle 0, -2 angle$.
questions.explanation: Use the formula $\cos 	heta =  rac{\mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{v}}{||\mathbf{u}|| ||\mathbf{v}||}$. The dot product is $(1)(0) + (0)(-2) = 0$. Since the numerator is 0, $\cos 	heta = 0$, which means $	heta =  rac{\pi}{2}$ (the vectors are orthogonal).

questions.question: Find the angle $	heta$ (in degrees) between the vectors: $\mathbf{u} = 3\mathbf{i} + 4\mathbf{j}$ and $\mathbf{v} = -7\mathbf{i} + 5\mathbf{j}$.
questions.explanation:
Calculate the dot product: $\mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{v} = (3)(-7) + (4)(5) = -21 + 20 = -1$. 
Calculate magnitudes: $||\mathbf{u}|| = \sqrt{3^2 + 4^2} = 5$, and $||\mathbf{v}|| = \sqrt{(-7)^2 + 5^2} = \sqrt{74}$. 
Apply the formula: $\cos 	heta =  rac{-1}{5\sqrt{74}} \approx -0.0232$. 
Using the inverse cosine function, $	heta = \arccos(-0.0232) \approx 91.3^\circ$.

questions.question: Find the angle $	heta$ (in degrees) between the vectors: $\mathbf{u} = 6\mathbf{i} - 3\mathbf{j}$ and $\mathbf{v} = -4\mathbf{i} - 4\mathbf{j}$.
questions.explanation:
Calculate the dot product: $\mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{v} = (6)(-4) + (-3)(-4) = -24 + 12 = -12$. 
Calculate magnitudes: $||\mathbf{u}|| = \sqrt{36 + 9} = \sqrt{45} = 3\sqrt{5}$, and $||\mathbf{v}|| = \sqrt{16 + 16} = \sqrt{32} = 4\sqrt{2}$. 
Apply the formula: $\cos 	heta =  rac{-12}{(3\sqrt{5})(4\sqrt{2})} =  rac{-12}{12\sqrt{10}} = - rac{1}{\sqrt{10}}$. 
Using the inverse cosine function, $	heta = \arccos(-1/\sqrt{10}) \approx 108.4^\circ$.

question_types.title: Determining Orthogonal Vectors
question_types.mapping_rationale: Determining if two vectors are orthogonal is a direct application of computing the dot product (PC.VM.1.1) and evaluating whether the resulting scalar simplifies exactly to zero using algebraic substitution (PC.ASE.2.1).

questions.question: Determine whether the vectors $\mathbf{u}$ and $\mathbf{v}$ are orthogonal: $\mathbf{u} = \langle 3, 15 angle, \mathbf{v} = \langle -1, 5 angle$.
questions.explanation:
Two vectors are orthogonal if their dot product is 0. 
$\mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{v} = (3)(-1) + (15)(5) = -3 + 75 = 72$. 
Since $72 
eq 0$, the vectors are not orthogonal.

questions.question: Determine whether the vectors $\mathbf{u}$ and $\mathbf{v}$ are orthogonal: $\mathbf{u} = 2\mathbf{i} - 2\mathbf{j}, \mathbf{v} = -\mathbf{i} - \mathbf{j}$.
questions.explanation:
Calculate the dot product: $\mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{v} = (2)(-1) + (-2)(-1) = -2 + 2 = 0$. 
Because the dot product is exactly 0, the vectors are orthogonal.

questions.question: Determine whether the vectors $\mathbf{u}$ and $\mathbf{v}$ are orthogonal: $\mathbf{u} = \langle \cos	heta, \sin	heta angle, \mathbf{v} = \langle \sin	heta, -\cos	heta angle$.
questions.explanation:
Calculate the dot product using the given algebraic expressions: 
$\mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{v} = (\cos	heta)(\sin	heta) + (\sin	heta)(-\cos	heta) = \cos	heta\sin	heta - \cos	heta\sin	heta = 0$. 
Since the dot product is 0 for any value of $	heta$, the vectors are always orthogonal.

question_types.title: Finding the Projection of u onto v
question_types.mapping_rationale: Calculating a vector projection directly models how forces or directional components are decomposed along a specific axis (PC.VM.2.1). The procedural execution requires chaining the dot product and scalar multiplication formulas (PC.VM.1.1).

questions.question: Find the projection of $\mathbf{u}$ onto $\mathbf{v}$: $\mathbf{u} = \langle 2, 2 angle$ and $\mathbf{v} = \langle 6, 1 angle$.
questions.explanation:
Use the projection formula: $	ext{proj}_{\mathbf{v}}\mathbf{u} = \left( rac{\mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{v}}{||\mathbf{v}||^2}ight)\mathbf{v}$. 
First, find the dot product: $\mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{v} = (2)(6) + (2)(1) = 14$. 
Next, find the square of the magnitude of $\mathbf{v}$: $||\mathbf{v}||^2 = 6^2 + 1^2 = 37$. 
Multiply the scalar $ rac{14}{37}$ by vector $\mathbf{v}$: $ rac{14}{37}\langle 6, 1 angle = \langle  rac{84}{37},  rac{14}{37} angle$.

questions.question: Find the projection of $\mathbf{u}$ onto $\mathbf{v}$: $\mathbf{u} = \langle 0, 3 angle$ and $\mathbf{v} = \langle 2, 15 angle$.
questions.explanation:
Calculate the dot product: $\mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{v} = (0)(2) + (3)(15) = 45$. 
Calculate $||\mathbf{v}||^2 = 2^2 + 15^2 = 4 + 225 = 229$. 
The projection is $ rac{45}{229} \langle 2, 15 angle = \langle  rac{90}{229},  rac{675}{229} angle$.

questions.question: Find the projection of $\mathbf{u}$ onto $\mathbf{v}$: $\mathbf{u} = \langle 4, 2 angle$ and $\mathbf{v} = \langle 1, -2 angle$.
questions.explanation:
Calculate the dot product: $\mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{v} = (4)(1) + (2)(-2) = 4 - 4 = 0$. 
Because the dot product is 0, the vectors are orthogonal. The projection of any vector onto a vector orthogonal to it is the zero vector, $\langle 0, 0 angle$.

question_types.title: Applications: Calculating Work using Vectors
question_types.mapping_rationale: Calculating the physical work done by a force over a distance is a classic vector application (PC.VM.2.1). Because the forces are applied at angles relative to the motion, students must use trigonometric modeling to isolate the parallel force component (PC.FT.4.1).

questions.question: A constant force of $45$ pounds, exerted at an angle of $30^\circ$ with the horizontal, is required to slide a desk across a floor. Determine the work done in sliding the desk $20$ feet.
questions.explanation:
Work is defined as $W = ||\mathbf{F}|| ||\overrightarrow{PQ}|| \cos	heta$. 
Substitute the given values: $W = (45)(20) \cos 30^\circ$. 
Using the exact unit circle value for $\cos 30^\circ$, $W = 900 \left( rac{\sqrt{3}}{2}ight) = 450\sqrt{3}$ foot-pounds (approximately 779.4 ft-lb).

questions.question: A tractor pulls a log 800 meters, and the tension in the cable connecting the tractor and the log is approximately 15,691 newtons. The direction of the constant force is $35^\circ$ above the horizontal. Determine the work done in pulling the log.
questions.explanation:
Use the work formula: $W = ||\mathbf{F}|| ||\overrightarrow{PQ}|| \cos	heta$. 
Substitute the known values: $W = (15691)(800) \cos 35^\circ$. 
$W = 12,552,800 \cos 35^\circ \approx 10,282,652$ Joules.
candidate_image_refs: Page 437 bbox [0.12, 0.17, 0.49, 0.28] — A diagram showing a tractor pulling a log with a force vector at 35 degrees.

questions.question: One of the events in a strength competition is to pull a cement block 100 feet. One competitor pulls the block by exerting a constant force of 250 pounds on a rope attached to the block at an angle of $30^\circ$ with the horizontal. Determine the work done in pulling the block.
questions.explanation:
Use the work formula: $W = ||\mathbf{F}|| ||\overrightarrow{PQ}|| \cos	heta$. 
Substitute the known values: $W = (250)(100) \cos 30^\circ$. 
Using the exact trig value, $W = 25000 \left( rac{\sqrt{3}}{2}ight) = 12500\sqrt{3}$ foot-pounds (approximately 21,650.6 ft-lb).
candidate_image_refs: Page 437 bbox [0.12, 0.45, 0.50, 0.55] — A diagram showing a man pulling a cement block with a rope at a 30-degree angle.

question_types.title: Sum and Difference Formulas Vocabulary
question_types.mapping_rationale: Directly assesses recall of the fundamental sum and difference formulas for sine, cosine, and tangent.

questions.question: Fill in the blank to complete the sum/difference formula: $\sin(u - v) = \text{\underline{\hspace{2cm}}}$
questions.explanation: The difference formula for sine is $\sin(u - v) = \sin u \cos v - \cos u \sin v$.

questions.question: Fill in the blank to complete the sum/difference formula: $\cos(u + v) = \text{\underline{\hspace{2cm}}}$
questions.explanation: The sum formula for cosine is $\cos(u + v) = \cos u \cos v - \sin u \sin v$.

questions.question: Fill in the blank to complete the sum/difference formula: $\tan(u + v) = \text{\underline{\hspace{2cm}}}$
questions.explanation: The sum formula for tangent is $\tan(u + v) = \frac{\tan u + \tan v}{1 - \tan u \tan v}$.

questions.question: Fill in the blank to complete the sum/difference formula: $\sin(u + v) = \text{\underline{\hspace{2cm}}}$
questions.explanation: The sum formula for sine is $\sin(u + v) = \sin u \cos v + \cos u \sin v$.

question_types.title: Rewriting and Evaluating Trigonometric Expressions
question_types.mapping_rationale: Requires recognizing expanded sum/difference formulas and condensing them into a single trigonometric evaluation.

questions.question: Write the expression as the sine, cosine, or tangent of an angle: $\sin 60^\circ \cos 15^\circ + \cos 60^\circ \sin 15^\circ$
questions.explanation: This follows the pattern $\sin u \cos v + \cos u \sin v = \sin(u + v)$. Thus, $\sin(60^\circ + 15^\circ) = \sin 75^\circ$.

questions.question: Write the expression as the sine, cosine, or tangent of an angle: $\cos 130^\circ \cos 40^\circ - \sin 130^\circ \sin 40^\circ$
questions.explanation: This follows the pattern $\cos u \cos v - \sin u \sin v = \cos(u + v)$. Thus, $\cos(130^\circ + 40^\circ) = \cos 170^\circ$.

questions.question: Find the exact value of the expression: $\cos \frac{\pi}{16} \cos \frac{3\pi}{16} - \sin \frac{\pi}{16} \sin \frac{3\pi}{16}$
questions.explanation: Using the sum formula for cosine: $\cos\left(\frac{\pi}{16} + \frac{3\pi}{16}\right) = \cos\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right) = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}$.

questions.question: Find the exact value of the expression: $\sin 100^\circ \cos 40^\circ - \cos 100^\circ \sin 40^\circ$
questions.explanation: Using the difference formula for sine: $\sin(100^\circ - 40^\circ) = \sin(60^\circ) = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$.

questions.question: Find the exact value of the expression: $\frac{\tan(9\pi/8) - \tan(\pi/8)}{1 + \tan(9\pi/8)\tan(\pi/8)}$
questions.explanation: Using the difference formula for tangent: $\tan\left(\frac{9\pi}{8} - \frac{\pi}{8}\right) = \tan(\pi) = 0$.

question_types.title: Evaluating Expressions with Given Conditions
question_types.mapping_rationale: Requires setting up multiple triangles based on quadrant constraints to find missing sine/cosine values, then applying sum/difference formulas.
